      add('difficulty', `must be one of: ${difficulties.join(', ')}`);
    }

    // highYield
    if (typeof q.highYield !== 'boolean') add('highYield', 'must be boolean');

    // stem
    if (typeof q.stem !== 'string' || q.stem.trim() === '') add('stem', 'must be non-empty string');

    // choices
    if (!Array.isArray(q.choices)) add('choices', 'must be an array');
    else {
      if (q.choices.length < 2 || q.choices.length > 10) add('choices', 'must have between 2 and 10 items');
      q.choices.forEach((c, i) => {
        if (typeof c !== 'string' || c.trim() === '') add(`choices[${i}]`, 'must be non-empty string');
      });
    }

    // answer: integer index or string matching a choice
    const ans = q.answer;
    if (typeof ans === 'number') {
      if (!Number.isInteger(ans) || ans < 0 || (Array.isArray(q.choices) && ans >= q.choices.length)) {
        add('answer', 'numeric answer must be integer within choices range (0-based)');
      }
    } else if (typeof ans === 'string') {
      const found = Array.isArray(q.choices) && q.choices.some(c => c === ans);
      if (!found) add('answer', 'string answer must match one of the choices exactly');
    } else {
      add('answer', 'must be an integer index or the exact choice string');
    }

    // explanation/trap/related
    ['explanation','trap','related'].forEach(f => {
      if (typeof q[f] !== 'string') add(f, 'must be a string (use empty string if none)');
    });

    // tags
    if (!Array.isArray(q.tags)) add('tags', 'must be an array (can be empty)');
    else {
      q.tags.forEach((t,i) => {
        if (typeof t !== 'string' || t.trim() === '') add(`tags[${i}]`, 'tag must be non-empty string');
      });
    }

    // image & reference: allow null or string (empty allowed)
    ['image','reference'].forEach(f => {
      if (!(q[f] === null || typeof q[f] === 'string')) add(f, 'must be a string or null');
    });

    // Optional: further checks (lengths)
    if (typeof q.stem === 'string' && q.stem.length > 5000) add('stem', 'very long stem (over 5000 chars)');
  });

  return { valid: errors.length === 0, errors };
}import { validateQuestions } from './schema-validator.js';

async function loadQuestions() {
  try {
    const resp = await fetch(DATA_PATH, { cache: "no-store" });
    const data = await resp.json();

    // validate
    const res = await validateQuestions(data);
    if (!res.valid) {
      // show validation errors to user (don't proceed to use the invalid data)

        '</ul>';
      document.getElementById('loader').appendChild(elErrors);
      return;
    }

    // proceed safely — data is valid
    questions = data;
    ...
  } catch (err) {
    // fallback file input, same validation after reading file
  }

    "choices": [
      "Plantar aponeurosis (fascia)",
      "Achilles tendon",
      "Tibialis posterior tendon",
      "Flexor digitorum brevis"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Plantar fasciitis involves microtears/inflammation of the plantar fascia inserting at the medial calcaneal tubercle, causing morning heel pain.",
    "trap": "Achilles tendon pain is posterior ankle/heel and aggravated by plantarflexion.",
    "related": "Stretching and orthotics are first-line management; corticosteroid injections are used cautiously.",
    "tags": ["foot","orthopedics","sports-medicine"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "A posterior knee dislocation compresses the structure deep within the popliteal fossa causing absent distal pulses and cool foot. Which structure is most at risk in this injury?",
    "choices": [
      "Popliteal artery",
      "Tibial nerve",
      "Small saphenous vein",
      "Femoral artery"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Posterior knee dislocations risk injury to the popliteal artery which lies deep in the popliteal fossa and supplies distal leg/pedal pulses.",
    "trap": "Tibial nerve injury causes neurological deficits but the immediate vascular emergency is popliteal artery injury.",
    "related": "Prompt vascular assessment and possible repair are critical to limb salvage.",

    "choices": [
      "Left main bronchus",
      "Right main bronchus",
      "Tracheal carina",
      "Lobar bronchus of the left lower lobe"
    ],
    "answer": 1,

    "trap": "In children anatomical proportions vary and foreign body location may differ.",
    "related": "Bronchoscopy is used to localize and remove aspirated objects.",

  },

    "stem": "During a gastroesophageal reflux workup, an imaging study shows a hiatal hernia where the stomach has herniated through the diaphragmatic opening that transmits the esophagus. What is the name and vertebral level of this opening?",
    "choices": [
      "Aortic hiatus at T12",
      "Esophageal hiatus at T10",
      "Caval opening at T8",
      "Sternal hiatus at T11"
    ],
    "answer": 1,
    "explanation": "The esophageal hiatus is at the T10 level and transmits the esophagus and vagal trunks; herniation through this opening causes hiatal hernia.",
    "trap": "Remember 'I 8 10 Eggs At 12' mnemonic: IVC at T8, esophagus at T10, aorta at T12.",
    "related": "Hiatal hernias contribute to reflux and may require surgical repair if symptomatic.",

  },

    "choices": [
      "Right ribs 6-8",
      "Left ribs 9-11",
      "Left ribs 4-6",
      "Right ribs 10-12"
    ],
    "answer": 1,
    "explanation": "The spleen is protected by the left ribs, particularly ribs 9-11; injuries to these ribs can be associated with splenic trauma.",
    "trap": "Do not confuse left and right; the liver is on the right side under ribs 7-10.",
    "related": "Splenic injuries risk internal bleeding and may require embolization or splenectomy.",

  },

    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A general surgeon uses McBurney's point to plan an appendectomy incision. Where is McBurney's point located?",
    "choices": [
      "Midpoint between umbilicus and left ASIS",
      "One-third from ASIS to umbilicus on the right",
      "At the midline two finger breadths above the pubic symphysis",
      "Directly over the right iliac crest"
    ],
    "answer": 1,
    "explanation": "McBurney's point is about one-third of the distance from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the umbilicus on the right and corresponds to the base of the appendix.",
    "trap": "Midline or left-sided points are not relevant for appendiceal localization.",
    "related": "Appendicitis classically presents with periumbilical pain migrating to right lower quadrant (McBurney's).",

    "stem": "A patient presents with diplopia; on exam, there is inability to abduct the right eye. Which cranial nerve is injured and which extraocular muscle is primarily affected?",
    "choices": [
      "CN VI; lateral rectus",
      "CN III; medial rectus",
      "CN IV; superior oblique",
      "CN III; inferior oblique"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "CN VI (abducens) innervates the lateral rectus muscle, which abducts the eye; injury causes inability to abduct and horizontal diplopia worse when looking toward the affected side.",
    "trap": "CN III palsy affects several muscles and causes more complex eye positions.",
    "related": "Increased intracranial pressure can stretch CN VI leading to palsy.",

  },

    "choices": [

      "Hypoglossal (CN XII) only",
      "Trigeminal (CN V) only",
      "Facial nerve (CN VII) and accessory (CN XI)"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Swallowing involves CN IX and X for pharyngeal sensation and motor function; lesions cause dysphagia and absent gag reflex.",
    "trap": "Hypoglossal affects tongue movement but primary pharyngeal function is CN IX/X.",
    "related": "Dysphagia post stroke often requires swallow assessment and possible modified diet.",

    "choices": [
      "Substantia nigra pars compacta (loss of dopaminergic neurons)",
      "Caudate nucleus neuronal loss",
      "Globus pallidus interna demyelination",
      "Thalamic infarct"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Parkinson disease is associated with loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta leading to basal ganglia circuitry dysfunction and classic motor signs.",
    "trap": "Caudate atrophy is more characteristic of Huntington disease.",
    "related": "Understanding basal ganglia circuits informs treatments like deep brain stimulation.",

    "topic": "Locked-in Syndrome",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A patient has preserved consciousness and vertical eye movements but quadriplegia and inability to speak after a pontine infarct. Which structure is most likely damaged, causing 'locked-in syndrome'?",
    "choices": [
      "Ventral pons (corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts)",
      "Midbrain reticular formation",
      "Thalamus",
      "Cerebellar vermis"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Ventral pontine lesions affecting corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts result in quadriplegia and loss of voluntary facial and speech movements while consciousness and vertical eye movement pathways remain intact.",
    "trap": "Midbrain lesions cause different syndromes (Weber, Claude) with oculomotor involvement but not the classic locked-in presentation.",
    "related": "Preserved vertical eye movements help distinguish locked-in from coma.",
    "tags": ["brainstem","stroke","critical-care"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [
      "Ophthalmic (V1)",
      "Maxillary (V2)",
      "Mandibular (V3)",
      "Facial nerve (VII)"
    ],
    "answer": 2,

    "trap": "Ophthalmic division is forehead/scalp; maxillary is midface/upper teeth.",

    "choices": [
      "Pelvic splanchnic nerves (S2-S4)",
      "Vagus nerve (CN X)",
      "Hypogastric nerve (sympathetic)",
      "Pudendal nerve"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Pelvic splanchnic nerves provide parasympathetic innervation to the detrusor muscle enabling reflexive bladder contraction; supraspinal control is impaired in higher lesions.",
    "trap": "Hypogastric is sympathetic and promotes urine retention; pudendal is somatic to external sphincter.",
    "related": "Spinal cord lesions can produce reflex bladder with incontinence due to loss of cortical control.",

    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "An elderly woman falls on an outstretched hand and suffers a distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement of the distal fragment. What is the eponymous name for this fracture pattern?",
    "choices": [
      "Smith fracture",
      "Colles fracture",
      "Scaphoid fracture",
      "Barton fracture"
    ],
    "answer": 1,
    "explanation": "A Colles fracture involves an extra-articular distal radius fracture with dorsal (posterior) displacement of the distal fragment typically from FOOSH in older patients.",
    "trap": "Smith fracture is a reverse Colles with volar (anterior) displacement.",
    "related": "Distal radius fractures can be associated with ulnar styloid fractures and require assessment of wrist stability.",

  },

    "choices": [

      "Lateral breast to axillary nodes; axillary nerve",
      "Upper outer quadrant to axillary nodes; radial nerve"
    ],

    "choices": [
      "Subcutaneous olecranon bursa",

      "Radioulnar bursa",
      "Bicipitoradial bursa"
    ],

    "trap": "Subtendinous bursa sits between triceps tendon and olecranon and is less commonly affected by superficial pressure.",

      "Medial circumflex femoral artery (retinacular branches)",
      "Superior gluteal artery",
      "Inferior gluteal artery",
      "Obturator artery"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Retinacular branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery supply most of the femoral head; intracapsular fractures disrupt these vessels risking avascular necrosis.",
    "trap": "Obturator artery supplies the ligamentum teres supply which is minor in adults; its contribution is limited.",

      "Directly into the external iliac vein",

    ],
    "answer": 1,

    "trap": "The small saphenous vein drains into the popliteal vein.",

    "choices": [
      "Aortic valve",
      "Mitral valve",
      "Tricuspid valve",
      "Pulmonary valve"
    ],

    "trap": "Tricuspid regurgitation is holosystolic but best heard at left lower sternal border and increases with inspiration.",

    "stem": "A patient presents with hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension after chest trauma. Which pericardial condition best explains these findings?",
    "choices": [
      "Constrictive pericarditis",
      "Cardiac tamponade (pericardial effusion under pressure)",

      "Pericardial effusion without hemodynamic compromise"
    ],

      "Malleus",

    ],

      "Sympathetic splanchnic nerves",
      "Somatic motor fibers",
      "Cervical sympathetic chain"
    ],

    "choices": [

      "Lateral cord",
      "Medial cord",
      "Upper trunk"
    ],

    ],

    "choices": [

    ],

    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Corticospinal tract damage (UMN lesion) produces spasticity, hyperreflexia, and Babinski sign (upgoing plantar).",
    "trap": "LMN lesions produce flaccid paralysis, fasciculations, and hyporeflexia.",
    "related": "Localization helps differentiate central from peripheral causes of weakness.",
    "tags": ["corticospinal","stroke","neuro"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {
    "id": "ANAT0078",
    "subject": "Autonomic Nervous System",
    "chapter": "Sympathetic Outflow",
    "topic": "Horner Syndrome",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A patient presents with ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis on one side of the face after a Pancoast tumor. Which sympathetic pathway is interrupted to cause Horner syndrome?",
    "choices": [
      "Cervical sympathetic trunk/postganglionic fibers to head",
      "Vagal parasympathetic fibers",
      "Phrenic nerve",
      "Inferior cervical ganglion exclusively"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Interruption of the sympathetic chain supplying the head (from hypothalamus through cervical sympathetic chain) causes Horner syndrome. Pancoast tumors at lung apex affect these sympathetic fibers.",
    "trap": "Phrenic nerve lesions cause diaphragmatic paralysis, not Horner syndrome.",
    "related": "Horner syndrome triad helps localize lesions along sympathetic pathway from hypothalamus to ocular structures.",
    "tags": ["horner-syndrome","sympathetic","oncology"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {
    "id": "ANAT0079",
    "subject": "General Anatomy",
    "chapter": "Fetal Circulation",
    "topic": "Ligament Remnants",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "In adults, the remnant of the umbilical vein within the falciform ligament is known by which name?",
    "choices": [
      "Ligamentum teres hepatis",
      "Ligamentum venosum",
      "Round ligament of bladder",
      "Median umbilical ligament"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The remnant of the fetal umbilical vein becomes the ligamentum teres hepatis (round ligament) found in the free margin of the falciform ligament.",
    "trap": "Ligamentum venosum is remnant of ductus venosus; median umbilical ligament is urachus remnant.",
    "related": "These embryological remnants are important surgical landmarks in hepatic surgery.",
    "tags": ["embryology","liver","anatomy"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {
    "id": "ANAT0080",
    "subject": "General Anatomy",
    "chapter": "Skin and Integument",
    "topic": "Dermatomes and Scalp Layers",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "A scalping injury separates scalp layers at the layer containing the loose connective tissue resulting in significant bleeding. Which scalp layer is the loose connective tissue layer?",
    "choices": [
      "Galea aponeurotica",
      "Loose areolar (connective) tissue layer",
      "Pericranium (pericranium)",
      "Skin"
    ],
    "answer": 1,
    "explanation": "The loose areolar connective tissue layer (the 'danger zone') allows the scalp to be degloved and harbors emissary veins causing significant bleeding and infection spread.",
    "trap": "Galea aponeurotica is the tough aponeurotic layer above the loose connective tissue.",
    "related": "Scalp wounds can track infections to intracranial venous sinuses via emissary veins.",
    "tags": ["scalp","surgical-anatomy"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {

    "chapter": "Elbow",

    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "A plumber presents with swelling over the posterior elbow after repeated pressure. Aspiration reveals fluid from a superficial sac over the olecranon. Which bursa is affected in 'student's elbow'?",
    "choices": [
      "Subcutaneous olecranon bursa",
      "Intratendinous bursa",
      "Subdeltoid bursa",
      "Subscapular bursa"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Subcutaneous olecranon bursa is superficial over the olecranon causing 'student's elbow' or olecranon bursitis due to repetitive pressure.",
    "trap": "Subdeltoid and subscapular bursae relate to shoulder not elbow.",
    "related": "Septation and infection must be considered with aspiration and culture.",

  },

    "stem": "A patient with progressive weakness of scapular stabilization has prominence of the medial border of the scapula when pushing on a wall. Which nerve injury is classically responsible for a winged scapula due to serratus anterior weakness?",
    "choices": [
      "Long thoracic nerve injury (C5-C7)",
      "Spinal accessory nerve injury",
      "Dorsal scapular nerve injury",
      "Thoracodorsal nerve injury"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Long thoracic nerve innervates serratus anterior; injury leads to medial scapular winging especially with pushing movements.",
    "trap": "Accessory nerve injury causes trapezius weakness and lateral scapular droop; dorsal scapular affects rhomboids.",
    "related": "Injury may result from axillary surgery or thoracostomy incisions.",

  },

      "Obturator nerve",
      "Sciatic nerve",
      "Superficial peroneal nerve"
    ],

    "related": "Adductor canal block provides analgesia for knee surgeries while sparing quadriceps motor function partially.",

    "choices": [
      "Intercostal nerves (ventral rami of thoracic spinal nerves)",
      "Dorsal rami of thoracic nerves",
      "Phrenic nerve",
      "Vagus nerve"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Intercostal muscles are supplied by intercostal nerves which are the ventral rami of thoracic spinal nerves; these provide motor and sensory innervation to the thoracic wall.",
    "trap": "Phrenic nerve innervates the diaphragm, not intercostals.",
    "related": "Intercostal nerve damage causes paradoxical breathing patterns and respiratory compromise.",

  },

    "choices": [
      "Diaphragmatic (central) pleura",
      "Costal pleura",
      "Mediastinal pleura",
      "Visceral pleura"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Irritation of the central diaphragmatic pleura is referred to the shoulder tip (C3-C5) via the phrenic nerve.",
    "trap": "Visceral pleura lacks somatic sensation and does not refer to shoulder tip.",
    "related": "Left shoulder pain from diaphragmatic irritation is Kehr's sign and may indicate splenic rupture or subdiaphragmatic processes.",

    "choices": [

      "Transverse colon and mesocolon",
      "Posterior abdominal wall",
      "Diaphragm"
    ],

    "explanation": "The stomach and lesser omentum (hepatogastric ligament) form the anterior boundary of the lesser sac (omental bursa); the lesser sac lies posterior to stomach.",
    "trap": "Transverse mesocolon forms the inferior boundary; posterior abdominal wall is the posterior boundary.",

    ],
    "answer": 2,
    "explanation": "The second part of duodenum lies lateral to head of pancreas and receives bile and pancreatic secretions at the major duodenal papilla (ampulla of Vater).",

  {

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "A patient squeezes a pimple in the 'danger triangle' of the face and later develops cavernous sinus thrombosis. Which venous connections explain spread of infection from face to cavernous sinus?",
    "choices": [
      "Facial vein communicating with superior ophthalmic vein/ cavernous sinus via angular vein (valveless)",
      "External jugular vein direct drainage to cavernous sinus",
      "Internal carotid arterial anastomoses",
      "Lymphatic drainage to deep cervical nodes"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The facial vein connects via angular and superior ophthalmic veins to the cavernous sinus; these valveless veins allow retrograde spread of infection causing cavernous sinus thrombosis.",
    "trap": "Arterial anastomoses do not explain venous spread; external jugular drains superficially and not to cavernous sinus.",
    "related": "Signs include periorbital edema, cranial nerve deficits, and severe systemic illness.",

  },
  {
    "id": "ANAT0089",
    "subject": "Cranial Nerves",
    "chapter": "Oculomotor Complex",
    "topic": "Pupil-sparing vs Pupil-involving",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A diabetic patient presents with ptosis and 'down and out' eye position but with preserved pupillary reflex. Which type of lesion (ischemic vs compressive) does this pattern suggest and why?",
    "choices": [
      "Ischemic microvascular CN III palsy sparing peripheral parasympathetic fibers (pupil spared)",
      "Compressive lesion (aneurysm) affecting peripheral fibers (pupil-involving)",
      "Lesion of CN IV causing pupillary involvement",
      "Lesion of optic nerve causing pupillary changes"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Ischemic (diabetic) CN III palsy typically affects the core somatic fibers but spares peripheral parasympathetic fibers (pupil), whereas compression (e.g., aneurysm) affects peripheral pupillary fibers first.",
    "trap": "Aneurysmal compression tends to involve pupillary fibers causing dilation.",
    "related": "Pupil involvement is a red flag for compressive lesions requiring imaging.",
    "tags": ["oculomotor","diabetes","neuro-ophthalmology"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "stem": "A stroke affecting the ventral posterolateral (VPL) nucleus of the thalamus produces contralateral loss of body sensation. Which ascending pathways synapse in the VPL nucleus?",
    "choices": [
      "Spinothalamic and dorsal column-medial lemniscus",
      "Optic radiations and auditory radiations",
      "Corticospinal tract",
      "Vestibulospinal tract"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The VPL nucleus receives somatosensory information from body via spinothalamic (pain/temperature) and dorsal column-medial lemniscus (touch/proprioception) pathways.",
    "trap": "Optic/auditory radiations synapse in lateral geniculate (LGN) and medial geniculate nuclei, not VPL.",
    "related": "Thalamic stroke causes hemisensory loss and sometimes central pain syndromes.",

    "choices": [

      "Hinge joint",
      "Pivot joint",
      "Saddle joint"

    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A young patient falls on an outstretched hand and has anatomical snuffbox tenderness. X-ray is initially negative. Which carpal bone is most suspected and what is the risk if diagnosis is delayed?",
    "choices": [
      "Scaphoid fracture; risk of avascular necrosis of proximal fragment",
      "Lunate dislocation; risk of carpal tunnel syndrome",
      "Hamate fracture; risk of ulnar nerve palsy",
      "Triquetrum fracture; risk of ulnar artery injury"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Scaphoid fractures are suspected with anatomical snuffbox tenderness; the proximal fragment has retrograde blood supply risking avascular necrosis if diagnosis and immobilization are delayed.",
    "trap": "Lunate dislocations produce carpal tunnel but snuffbox tenderness is classic for scaphoid.",
    "related": "High index of suspicion may prompt repeat imaging or MRI to detect occult scaphoid fractures.",

    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [
      "Dupuytren contracture (palmar fibromatosis)",
      "Trigger finger",
      "Flexor tenosynovitis",
      "Carpal tunnel syndrome"
    ],

    "explanation": "Dupuytren contracture is palmar fascial fibrosis leading to flexion contractures usually in ring and little fingers.",
    "trap": "Trigger finger involves tendon pulley; Dupuytren is fascial thickening.",

    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "An athlete has progressive calf pain after a tibial fracture with decreased dorsiflexion and tense leg compartments on exam. What is the most concerning early sign of acute compartment syndrome?",
    "choices": [
      "Pain out of proportion to exam and pain on passive stretch",
      "Loss of deep tendon reflexes",
      "Cold and mottled skin late sign",
      "Shiny skin only"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Severe pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch are early and sensitive signs of acute compartment syndrome, requiring urgent fasciotomy.",
    "trap": "Late signs include pulselessness and pallor, but waiting for these can risk necrosis.",
    "related": "Monitoring compartment pressures can aid diagnosis when clinical uncertainty exists.",

    "reference": null
  },

    ],

      "Meningocele",
      "Leptomeningeal cyst"
    ],

    ],

      "C7-C8",
      "T1-T2",
      "C3-C4"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Elbow flexion (biceps) and lateral forearm sensation are supplied by C5-C6 roots (musculocutaneous and lateral cutaneous nerve of forearm); injuries produce Erb-like deficits.",
    "trap": "C7-C8 injuries affect triceps and hand intrinsic muscles rather than elbow flexion primarily.",

  },
  {
    "id": "ANAT0104",
    "subject": "Upper Limb",
    "chapter": "Shoulder",
    "topic": "Scapular Motion",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "During abduction of the arm above the horizontal plane, the scapula rotates to allow full range. Which muscle primarily upwardly rotates the scapula?",
    "choices": [
      "Serratus anterior",
      "Rhomboid major",
      "Latissimus dorsi",
      "Pectoralis minor"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Serratus anterior (with upper and lower trapezius) upwardly rotates the scapula, essential for full arm abduction.",
    "trap": "Rhomboids retract and downwardly rotate the scapula rather than upwardly rotate.",
    "related": "Serratus anterior dysfunction (long thoracic nerve palsy) leads to winged scapula and impaired abduction.",
    "tags": ["scapula","shoulder","muscles"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "topic": "Bicipital Aponeurosis",
    "difficulty": "Hard",

    "stem": "A clinician performing a venipuncture near the cubital fossa is aware of structures deep to the bicipital aponeurosis. Which of the following structures lies deep to the aponeurosis and is at risk in deep procedures?",
    "choices": [
      "Brachial artery and median nerve",
      "Radial collateral artery only",
      "Cephalic vein",
      "Ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The bicipital aponeurosis overlies the brachial artery and median nerve; deep penetration risks vascular or neural injury.",
    "trap": "Cephalic vein is superficial; ulnar nerve lies posterior to the medial epicondyle.",
    "related": "Brachial artery divides into radial and ulnar arteries below the elbow; care is needed during arterial puncture.",

  },

    "chapter": "Wrist",
    "topic": "Guyon's Canal",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "A bicyclist experiences numbness and weakness in the ulnar side of the hand after prolonged pressure on the handlebars. Which structure transmits the ulnar nerve into the hand within Guyon's canal?",
    "choices": [
      "Pisiform and hook of hamate forming canal",
      "Flexor retinaculum solely",
      "Palmar aponeurosis",
      "Transverse carpal ligament lateral border"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Guyon's canal is bounded by the pisiform and hook of hamate and transmits the ulnar nerve and artery into the hand; compression causes ulnar neuropathy.",
    "trap": "Transverse carpal ligament relates to carpal tunnel, not Guyon's canal.",
    "related": "Cyclist's palsy is common due to pressure on ulnar canal structures.",
    "tags": ["guyons-canal","ulnar-nerve","hand"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A young athlete has recurrent lateral patellar dislocations related to maltracking. Which anatomical factor commonly predisposes to lateral patellar instability?",
    "choices": [
      "Shallow trochlear groove (trochlear dysplasia)",
      "Medial patellar facet hypertrophy",
      "Short quadriceps tendon",
      "Enhanced medial retinaculum strength"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Trochlear dysplasia (a shallow trochlear groove) reduces patellar stability and predisposes to lateral dislocation, especially with valgus stress and shallow trochlea.",
    "trap": "Enhanced medial structures would stabilize the patella; lateral instability relates to lateral pull and trochlear shape.",
    "related": "Surgical trochleoplasty or MPFL reconstruction may address severe instability.",

    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [
      "Sciatic nerve",
      "Femoral nerve",
      "Superior gluteal nerve",
      "Obturator nerve"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The sciatic nerve exits the pelvis below piriformis and courses posterior to the hip joint making it susceptible to injury with posterior dislocation.",
    "trap": "Femoral and obturator nerves are anterior/medial to the hip and less commonly injured in posterior dislocations.",
    "related": "Sciatic nerve injury can cause foot drop and sensory deficits in the posterolateral leg.",

  },

    ],

      "Ileocolic junction between SMA branches",
      "Rectosigmoid junction between internal iliac branches",
      "Cecal tip supplied by ileocolic artery"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The splenic flexure (Griffith's point) lies at the border zone between SMA and IMA perfusion and is vulnerable to watershed ischemia.",
    "trap": "Rectosigmoid is also watershed (Sudeck's point), but splenic flexure is classic for SMA/IMA watershed.",

    "choices": [

      "Inferior mesenteric artery (IMA)",
      "Celiac trunk",
      "Internal iliac artery"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The superior mesenteric artery supplies the midgut including jejunum and ileum; occlusion leads to intestinal ischemia and severe pain.",
    "trap": "Celiac trunk supplies foregut (stomach, duodenum up to major papilla); IMA supplies hindgut.",
    "related": "Acute mesenteric ischemia is a surgical emergency with high morbidity.",

    ],
    "answer": 0,

      "Spinothalamic tract",

      "Trigeminal nerve (CN V)",
      "Hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)"
    ],

    "explanation": "CN IX transmits baroreceptor information from the carotid sinus to the nucleus tractus solitarius, modulating heart rate reflexively.",

      "Caval opening at T8",

    ],

    "stem": "A patient with gallstones has right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right scapula after fatty meals. Which nerve-mediated pattern explains referred pain to the scapular region from gallbladder pathology?",
    "choices": [
      "Phrenic nerve (C3-C5) referred pain to shoulder/scapula via diaphragmatic irritation",
      "Intercostal nerves referring to the scapula",
      "Lumbar plexus referred pain to lower back",
      "Vagus nerve causing cranial referred pain"
    ],

      "Meckel diverticulum",

    ],

    ],

    "choices": [
      "Dopamine (D2 receptor blockade in basal ganglia)",
      "Acetylcholine (muscarinic blockade)",
      "Serotonin (5-HT blockade)",
      "GABAergic enhancement"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Dopamine D2 receptor blockade in basal ganglia pathways by antipsychotics can cause extrapyramidal side effects including tardive dyskinesia.",
    "trap": "Muscarinic blockade causes anticholinergic side effects but different movement disorders.",
    "related": "Treatment involves lowering offending agent or using VMAT2 inhibitors in severe tardive dyskinesia.",
    "tags": ["basal-ganglia","pharmacology","movement-disorders"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [

      "Tegmen tympani",
      "Mastoid antrum",
      "Auditory tube"
    ],

    "trap": "Tegmen tympani is the roof of middle ear separating it from middle cranial fossa.",
    "related": "Cholesteatoma may present with chronic otorrhea and conductive hearing loss.",

  },

    "chapter": "Sympathetic Chain",
    "topic": "Horner Syndrome",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A Pancoast tumor at the lung apex compresses sympathetic outflow causing ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis. Which cervical ganglion involvement explains ipsilateral facial anhidrosis in Horner syndrome?",
    "choices": [
      "Superior cervical ganglion interruption affecting sweat fibers to face",
      "Stellate ganglion lesion producing contralateral effects",
      "Middle cervical ganglion is responsible for facial sweating",
      "Thoracic sympathetic ganglia only"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The superior cervical ganglion provides postganglionic sympathetic fibers to the head and face including sweat fibers; its interruption yields facial anhidrosis.",
    "trap": "Stellate ganglion can cause Horner but superior cervical specifically gives facial sympathetic fibers.",
    "related": "Horner syndrome localization distinguishes central, preganglionic, and postganglionic lesions.",
    "tags": ["horner-syndrome","sympathetic-chain","oncology"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

      "Gallbladder anterior to pancreas"

    "choices": [

      "Hearing via cochlea function",
      "Sensation from posterior tongue",

    ],

    "related": "Otologic procedures must identify and preserve chorda tympani where possible to prevent gustatory dysfunction.",

      "Femoral artery alone"
    ],

    "choices": [

      "Insert needle at the midline sternum",

    ],

    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "During laparoscopic cholecystectomy, identification of Calot's triangle is essential to avoid bile duct injury. What forms the boundaries of Calot's (hepatocystic) triangle?",
    "choices": [

      "Portal vein, hepatic artery, common bile duct",
      "Gallbladder fundus, epigastric vessels, pylorus",
      "Right hepatic vein, cystic duct, duodenum"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Calot's triangle is bounded by the cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and the inferior surface of the liver (or more precisely cystic artery runs within it); correct identification prevents ductal injury.",
    "trap": "Portal triad includes portal vein and hepatic artery but not Calot's boundaries.",
    "related": "Critical view of safety technique avoids bile duct injuries during cholecystectomy.",

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A straddle injury results in extravasation of urine accumulating superficial to the perineal fascia. Which fascial layer prevents spread of urine into the thigh?",
    "choices": [
      "Colles' fascia (superficial perineal fascia) attached to fascia lata prevents spread to thigh",
      "Deep fascia of thigh (fascia lata) allows spread freely",
      "Scarpa's fascia in abdomen directs fluid to thigh",
      "Perineal body prevents all spread"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Colles' fascia fuses with fascia lata at the inguinal ligament and prevents superficial perineal fluid from spreading into the thigh; extravasation collects in superficial perineal spaces.",
    "trap": "Scarpa's fascia is continuous with Colles but perineal fluids are confined by attachments, not spread into thigh compartments.",
    "related": "Urethral injury patterns guide imaging (retrograde urethrography) and management.",

  }

      "Median nerve",

      "Musculocutaneous nerve"

      "Ulnar artery at Guyon's canal",

      "Ischial tuberosity avulsion"

    "stem": "Which mnemonic correctly lists structures in the femoral triangle from lateral to medial?",
    "choices": [
      "Nerve, Artery, Vein, Lymphatics (NAVL)",
      "Vein, Artery, Nerve (VAN)",
      "Artery only (A)",
      "Nerve, Vein, Artery (NVA)"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The femoral triangle contents from lateral to medial are Nerve (femoral), Artery (femoral), Vein (femoral), and Lymphatics (femoral canal) — NAVL.",
    "trap": "VAN is a common alternative mnemonic but orientation varies; NAVL is standard lateral-to-medial ordering.",
    "related": "Femoral artery catheterization uses this anatomical relation.",
    "tags": ["femoral-triangle","anatomical-mnemonic"],

    "reference": null
  },

    "stem": "A blow to the lateral knee producing valgus stress damages structures resisting this force. Which ligament primarily resists valgus stress at the knee?",
    "choices": [
      "Medial collateral ligament (MCL)",
      "Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)",
      "Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL)",
      "Posterior cruciate ligament (PCL)"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "The MCL resists valgus stress (force pushing the knee medially) and is commonly injured in contact sports.",
    "trap": "LCL resists varus stress; cruciate ligaments control anteroposterior stability.",
    "related": "MCL injuries can occur with concomitant meniscal damage (unhappy triad).",

  },

    "stem": "A middle-aged patient with myasthenia gravis has anterior mediastinal widening on chest x-ray. Which tumor should be suspected and is associated with MG?",
    "choices": [
      "Thymoma",
      "Bronchogenic carcinoma",
      "Teratoma",
      "Lymphoma only"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Thymoma in the anterior mediastinum is frequently associated with myasthenia gravis and can present as a mediastinal mass.",
    "trap": "Other anterior mediastinal masses (teratoma, lymphoma) are in differential but thymoma has a known MG association.",
    "related": "Thymectomy can improve MG symptoms in selected cases.",

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "An elderly patient with atrial fibrillation has sudden severe abdominal pain but minimal abdominal tenderness. Occlusion of which artery is classically implicated in acute mesenteric ischemia producing pain out of proportion?",
    "choices": [
      "Superior mesenteric artery (SMA)",
      "Inferior mesenteric artery (IMA)",
      "Celiac trunk",
      "Internal iliac artery"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Acute SMA occlusion causes midgut ischemia with severe pain disproportionate to physical findings because ischemia precedes peritonitis.",
    "trap": "IMA occlusion affects hindgut and causes different presentations; celiac trunk occlusion is rarer.",
    "related": "Early diagnosis with CT angiography is critical for revascularization and bowel salvage.",

  },

      "Transitional zone (central around urethra)",
      "Fibromuscular stroma",
      "Urethral mucosa"
    ],
    "answer": 0,

    "trap": "BPH causes urinary obstruction and arises in transitional zone not typically malignant.",
    "related": "PSA and DRE findings guide prostate cancer workup and biopsy.",

    "topic": "Glossopharyngeal Neuralgia",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Severe throat and ear pain triggered by swallowing suggests glossopharyngeal neuralgia. Which nerve transmits sensation from the oropharynx and posterior tongue and when irritated can cause such pain?",
    "choices": [
      "Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)",
      "Trigeminal nerve V1 division",
      "Vagus nerve CN X superficial branches",
      "Hypoglossal nerve CN XII"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "CN IX carries sensory fibers from oropharynx and posterior 1/3 tongue and can cause sharp paroxysmal pain in glossopharyngeal neuralgia.",
    "trap": "Trigeminal branch V1 provides forehead and corneal sensation; vagus involvement produces different symptoms.",
    "related": "Management includes anticonvulsants and microvascular decompression if refractory.",

    "reference": null
  },

    "difficulty": "Medium",

    "stem": "A patient demonstrates a tremor that worsens with purposeful movement and improves at rest. Damage to which part of the nervous system typically causes an intention tremor?",
    "choices": [
      "Cerebellar hemisphere",
      "Basal ganglia",
      "Peripheral nerves",
      "Motor cortex only"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Intention tremor is characteristic of cerebellar dysfunction (often hemisphere lesions) and becomes worse with target-directed movements.",
    "trap": "Basal ganglia causes resting tremor (Parkinsonian), not intention tremor.",
    "related": "Cerebellar lesions also cause dysmetria and ataxia.",

  },

    "stem": "A patient presents with unilateral facial weakness sparing the forehead. Which lesion localization is most consistent with this finding?",
    "choices": [
      "Upper motor neuron lesion (central) contralateral to weakness",
      "Lower motor neuron lesion of facial nerve ipsilateral",
      "Lesion of vestibulocochlear nerve",
      "Lesion of trigeminal nerve"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Forehead muscles receive bilateral cortical innervation; an UMN lesion causes contralateral lower facial weakness sparing forehead, distinguishing from peripheral (LMN) facial palsy which affects whole side.",
    "trap": "LMN (Bell's palsy) affects both upper and lower facial muscles on same side.",
    "related": "Stroke evaluation is indicated when forehead is spared in facial weakness.",

  },

    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Which reflex increases vagal parasympathetic activity resulting in sudden bradycardia when carotid sinus baroreceptors are stimulated?",
    "choices": [
      "Carotid sinus reflex via CN IX afferent and CN X efferent",
      "Cough reflex via vagus",
      "Gag reflex via CN X afferent",
      "Micturition reflex via pelvic splanchnics"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Carotid sinus baroreceptor activation sends afferent signals via CN IX to nucleus tractus solitarius, increasing vagal output via CN X, causing bradycardia and reduced sympathetic tone.",
    "trap": "Cough reflex is vagally mediated but not the primary baroreflex.",
    "related": "Carotid sinus hypersensitivity may cause syncope with neck manipulation.",
    "tags": ["baroreflex","vagus","autonomics"],

    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [

      "The largest lymph node in the basin",
      "Any palpable lymph node near tumor",
      "The most distal lymph node in the chain"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Sentinel node is the initial lymph node that receives lymphatic drainage from a tumor and is sampled to assess metastatic spread.",
    "trap": "Size or palpability does not define sentinel status; mapping techniques identify the sentinel node.",
    "related": "Sentinel node biopsy reduces morbidity by limiting nodal dissection if negative.",

  },

      "Gluteal abduction"

    "choices": [

      "Superior laryngeal nerve (external branch)",
      "Hypoglossal nerve",
      "Glossopharyngeal nerve"
    ],

      "Subdural hygroma",
      "Intracerebral hemorrhage"
    ],

      "Facial expression"

      "Bronchial arteries exclusively",

    ],

      "Trigeminal nerve (CN V1)",

    "stem": "Small vessel disease causing pure motor hemiparesis often produces lacunar infarcts in which location?",
    "choices": [
      "Posterior limb of internal capsule",
      "Primary visual cortex",
      "Cerebellar hemisphere",
      "Anterior frontal lobe cortex"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Lacunar infarcts affecting the posterior limb of the internal capsule disrupt corticospinal fibers producing pure motor hemiparesis.",
    "trap": "Cortical infarcts produce cortical signs like aphasia or visual field cuts; lacunar syndrome is subcortical.",
    "related": "Hypertension and diabetes are major risk factors for lacunar infarcts.",

  },

    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "Which anatomical difference explains why the left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under the aortic arch while the right loops under the subclavian artery?",
    "choices": [

      "Right vagus nerve lacks recurrent branch",
      "Left recurrent laryngeal nerve supplies different muscles",
      "Random anatomical variation in each person"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Embryological development of the aortic arches and differential descent of great vessels result in the left recurrent laryngeal nerve looping under the aortic arch while the right loops under the right subclavian.",
    "trap": "It's not random variation; it's a predictable embryological pattern.",
    "related": "Left recurrent laryngeal nerve is at risk in thoracic surgeries and aortic arch pathologies.",
    "tags": ["recurrent-laryngeal","embryology","vagus"],

      "Hypoglossal canal",

    ],

    "related": "Jugular foramen syndrome from tumors or infection demands neuroimaging.",

    "choices": [
      "Autosomal dominant CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion",
      "Autosomal recessive point mutation",
      "Mitochondrial inheritance",
      "X-linked recessive deletion"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Huntington disease is autosomal dominant due to expanded CAG repeats in HTT gene causing striatal degeneration and characteristic chorea and dementia.",
    "trap": "It is not autosomal recessive or mitochondrial; inheritance pattern is key for family counseling.",
    "related": "Anticipation leads to earlier onset in successive generations.",

    "difficulty": "Hard",

    "stem": "A patient with severe aortic stenosis develops reduced stroke volume despite preserved myocardial contractility. How does the increased afterload of aortic stenosis affect ventricular function acutely?",
    "choices": [
      "Decreases stroke volume for given contractility by increasing wall stress",
      "Increases stroke volume by increasing preload",
      "Has no effect on stroke volume",
      "Improves ejection fraction due to ventricular hypertrophy"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Increased afterload (outflow resistance) raises ventricular wall stress, reducing stroke volume for a given contractility; concentric hypertrophy may develop chronically but does not acutely increase stroke volume.",
    "trap": "Confusing chronic compensatory hypertrophy with acute hemodynamic effect; immediate afterload rise lowers stroke volume.",
    "related": "Afterload reduction (vasodilators) can improve forward flow in some HF states but is limited in fixed stenosis.",
    "tags": ["afterload","aortic-stenosis","heart-failure"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "A pulmonary embolus blocks blood flow to a lung segment. How does this affect local V/Q ratio and arterial oxygenation?",
    "choices": [
      "V/Q ratio increases (dead space) and causes arterial hypoxemia",
      "V/Q ratio decreases (shunt) and increases PaO2",
      "No change in V/Q",
      "Perfusion increases in the area"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Pulmonary embolus obstructs perfusion making ventilation without perfusion (physiologic dead space), increasing local V/Q and causing hypoxemia due to wasted ventilation and redistribution effects.",
    "trap": "Shunt implies perfusion without ventilation (low V/Q), opposite of embolus physiology.",
    "related": "PE may cause hypoxemia out of proportion to CXR findings; V/Q scan or CT angiography used diagnostically.",
    "tags": ["v/q","pulmonary-embolism","gas-exchange"],

    "choices": [
      "Increased glomerular permeability to proteins due to GBM or podocyte injury",
      "Increased plasma oncotic pressure preventing filtration",
      "Decreased glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure only",
      "Increased proximal tubule reabsorption of protein"
    ],
    "answer": 0,

    "trap": "Assuming oncotic or hydrostatic changes alone cause massive proteinuria neglects barrier integrity role.",

    "highYield": true,

    "choices": [
      "Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) ingestion which reduces Fe3+ to Fe2+",
      "High dietary calcium intake",
      "Proton pump inhibitor use (reduced acid)",
      "Phytate-rich diet"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Vitamin C enhances nonheme iron absorption by reducing ferric (Fe3+) to ferrous (Fe2+) iron and forming soluble complexes; calcium, PPIs, and phytates inhibit absorption.",
    "trap": "PPIs reduce gastric acidity needed for iron solubilization, decreasing absorption.",

    ],

      "Platelet count reduction only",
      "Enhancement of fibrinolysis"

    ],

    "choices": [

      "Decreased FRC and increased expiratory flow",
      "No change in FRC but improved flow",
      "Immediate alveolar collapse at rest"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Loss of elastic recoil in emphysema raises FRC (air trapping) and reduces driving pressure for passive expiration, promoting dynamic airway collapse and reduced expiratory flow (obstructive physiology).",

      "Hypothalamus increases TRH due to high T4",
      "TSH is suppressed by lack of thyroid hormone"
    ],

    "choices": [
      "Promote secretory changes and stabilize endometrium for potential implantation",
      "Induce ovulation directly",
      "Stimulate endometrial shedding",
      "Increase uterine contractility strongly"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Progesterone converts the proliferative endometrium into a secretory, receptive state and stabilizes it to support possible embryo implantation; withdrawal leads to menses.",
    "trap": "Progesterone does not induce ovulation; LH surge triggers ovulation.",
    "related": "Luteal phase defects (insufficient progesterone) can cause infertility or early pregnancy loss.",
    "tags": ["progesterone","menstrual-cycle","reproductive"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {
    "id": "PHYS0066",
    "subject": "Hematology",
    "chapter": "Blood Gas Transport",
    "topic": "Bohr Effect",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "A patient exercising vigorously produces lactic acid causing local acidosis in muscle. How does the Bohr effect improve oxygen delivery to active tissues?",
    "choices": [
      "Low pH shifts hemoglobin curve right, decreasing O2 affinity and enhancing unloading",
      "Low pH increases hemoglobin affinity for O2, reducing unloading",
      "pH has no effect on hemoglobin O2 binding",
      "Bohr effect increases O2 content directly"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Acidosis (low pH) lowers hemoglobin's O2 affinity (right shift) facilitating oxygen unloading in metabolically active tissues (Bohr effect).",
    "trap": "Opposite statement is incorrect; left shift increases affinity and hinders unloading.",
    "related": "Hypercapnia also contributes to right shift aiding O2 delivery.",
    "tags": ["bohr-effect","oxygen-delivery","exercise"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "chapter": "Mitochondria",

    "difficulty": "Hard",

    "stem": "Uncoupling proteins increase proton leak across inner mitochondrial membrane. What is the effect of uncoupling on ATP synthesis and heat production?",
    "choices": [
      "Decreased ATP production and increased heat generation",
      "Increased ATP production and decreased heat",
      "No change in ATP or heat",
      "Increased ATP without changing proton gradient"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Uncoupling dissipates proton gradient as heat, reducing ATP synthesis efficiency; thermogenesis (e.g., brown adipose tissue via UCP-1) increases heat production at expense of ATP.",
    "trap": "Assuming uncoupling increases ATP is incorrect: it reduces ATP yield per substrate oxidized.",
    "related": "Chemical uncouplers (2,4-DNP) cause hyperthermia and fatal metabolic derangement.",
    "tags": ["mitochondria","uncoupling","metabolism"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

      "Cardiac pump failure only",
      "Mechanical obstruction of venous return",
      "Loss of blood volume from hemorrhage"
    ],

      "Increased alveolar surface tension",
      "Decrease in respiratory rate"
    ],

    "related": "Bronchodilators (beta-2 agonists) restore airway radius reducing resistance.",

    "choices": [
      "Alanine (via the alanine cycle)",
      "Lactate cannot be used",
      "Free fatty acids converted directly to glucose",
      "Cholesterol"
    ],

    ],

    "explanation": "Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid and aortic bodies) sense hypoxemia (low PaO2) and increase ventilation; carotid bodies are primary O2 sensors located at carotid bifurcation.",

    "related": "Chronic hypercapnia can blunt central drive making hypoxic drive clinically important but variable.",

  },

    ],

      "Ectodermal origin unconnected to autonomic system"
    ],

    "trap": "They are not endodermal or regulated by TSH; this is a sympathetic endocrine interface.",
    "related": "Pheochromocytomas are catecholamine-secreting tumors of chromaffin cells.",

      "Increased hemoglobin synthesis"
    ],

    "trap": "CO poisoning yields cherry-red blood and different mechanism; 2,3-BPG shifts affinity differently.",
    "related": "Treatment includes methylene blue to reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ when not G6PD deficient.",

      "Ryanodine-independent release",
      "Sodium-induced sodium release",
      "Passive diffusion of K+"
    ],

    "choices": [

      "Paralytic ileus has focal luminal obstruction producing hyperperistalsis proximally",
      "Obstruction results from neural inhibition of peristalsis diffusely",
      "Ileus increases secretions causing rapid diarrhea"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Paralytic ileus is global hypomotility (commonly postoperative or metabolic) without mechanical blockage, whereas mechanical obstruction has a focal blockage often with hyperactive proximal peristalsis early.",

    "related": "Management of ileus includes bowel rest, correction of electrolytes, and mobilization.",

    "stem": "A patient with type 1 diabetes presents with polyuria and Kussmaul respirations. How does insulin deficiency result in ketoacidosis (DKA)?",
    "choices": [
      "Reduced insulin increases lipolysis and free fatty acids which the liver converts to ketone bodies",
      "Insulin deficiency promotes glycogen synthesis producing ketones",
      "Excess insulin causes ketosis",
      "Insulin deficiency increases peripheral glucose uptake preventing ketones"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Insulin deficiency releases inhibition of hormone-sensitive lipase increasing lipolysis; free fatty acids are oxidized in liver to ketone bodies (acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate) causing metabolic acidosis (DKA).",
    "trap": "Excess insulin would prevent lipolysis; glycogen synthesis is insulin-mediated, not ketogenic.",
    "related": "DKA management includes insulin, fluids, and electrolyte replacement (esp. potassium).",

  },

    ],

    "choices": [

      "Immediate increase in protein synthesis",
      "Mitochondrial DNA replication",
      "Plasma membrane depolarization"
    ],

    ],

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Familial hypercholesterolemia involves defective LDL receptor-mediated uptake. Which cellular process is impaired leading to elevated plasma LDL cholesterol?",
    "choices": [
      "Receptor-mediated endocytosis of LDL particles by hepatocytes via clathrin-coated pits",
      "Bulk-phase pinocytosis equally handling LDL",
      "Exocytosis of LDL increasing plasma LDL clearance",
      "Glycolysis in hepatocytes"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "LDL uptake requires receptor-mediated endocytosis (clathrin-dependent) by hepatocytes; defective or absent LDL receptors prevent cellular uptake causing high plasma LDL and premature atherosclerosis.",
    "trap": "Pinocytosis is nonspecific and not the main LDL uptake pathway; exocytosis would not clear LDL.",
    "related": "Statins increase LDL receptor expression to lower LDL cholesterol.",
    "tags": ["ldl-receptor","endocytosis","lipid-metabolism"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {
    "id": "PHYS0118",

    "chapter": "Autonomic",
    "topic": "Vagal Effects",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Vagal stimulation affects heart rate and AV nodal conduction. Which receptor and second messenger pathway mediate parasympathetic slowing of the heart via the vagus nerve?",
    "choices": [
      "Muscarinic M2 receptors decreasing cAMP via Gi protein",
      "Beta-1 receptors increasing cAMP",
      "Alpha-1 receptors via IP3",
      "Dopamine D2 receptors increasing cAMP"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Vagal acetylcholine binds M2 muscarinic receptors coupled to Gi, which reduces cAMP, decreasing pacemaker current and AV nodal conduction slowing heart rate.",
    "trap": "Beta-1 increases cAMP and heart rate (sympathetic), opposite to parasympathetic action.",
    "related": "Atropine blocks muscarinic receptors to treat bradycardia.",
    "tags": ["vagus","muscarinic","cardiac-autonomics"],
    "image": null,

  },

    "topic": "Vascular Tone",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Endothelial dysfunction reduces nitric oxide (NO) availability. How does reduced NO affect vascular smooth muscle and blood pressure?",
    "choices": [
      "Reduced NO decreases cGMP in smooth muscle causing vasoconstriction and increased blood pressure",
      "Reduced NO causes vasodilation and decreased blood pressure",
      "NO has no role in vascular tone",
      "NO increases intracellular Ca2+ to cause vasoconstriction"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Endothelial NO stimulates guanylate cyclase in smooth muscle increasing cGMP and causing relaxation; reduced NO leads to vasoconstriction, increased SVR, and hypertension tendency.",
    "trap": "Assuming NO causes constriction is incorrect; NO is a vasodilator.",
    "related": "Endothelial dysfunction is an early event in atherosclerosis and vascular disease.",
    "tags": ["endothelium","no","vascular"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

      "Alpha-2 receptor decreasing cAMP",
      "Muscarinic receptor activation decreasing heart rate only",

    ],

    "chapter": "Pulmonary Circulation",
    "topic": "Hypoxic Pulmonary Vasoconstriction",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) diverts blood away from poorly ventilated lung regions. What is the adaptive purpose of HPV and what happens with global alveolar hypoxia (as at high altitude)?",
    "choices": [
      "HPV optimizes V/Q matching regionally; global hypoxia increases pulmonary arterial pressure causing pulmonary hypertension",
      "HPV increases shunt to poorly ventilated areas only",
      "HPV reduces systemic blood pressure directly",
      "HPV has no physiological role"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "HPV is a local mechanism to divert blood from hypoxic alveoli improving V/Q matching; when hypoxia is global (e.g., altitude), widespread HPV increases pulmonary vascular resistance and can cause pulmonary hypertension and right heart strain.",
    "trap": "HPV improves, not worsens, V/Q matching locally; global activation leads to detrimental pulmonary hypertension.",
    "related": "High-altitude pulmonary edema relates to exaggerated HPV responses.",
    "tags": ["hpv","v/q","pulmonary-hypertension"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [

      "Act on loop of Henle increasing Ca2+ reabsorption",
      "Act on proximal tubule increasing Ca2+ excretion",
      "Act on collecting duct to promote Ca2+ loss"
    ],

    "related": "Thiazides used to treat idiopathic hypercalciuria and kidney stones.",

    ],

      "Decreased distal Na+ reabsorption causing hyperkalemia",
      "Reduced aldosterone increases K+ secretion",
      "Aldosterone has no effect on potassium"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Aldosterone increases ENaC activity and Na+/K+ ATPase in principal cells increasing Na+ reabsorption and creating a lumen-negative potential that drives K+ secretion, causing hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis.",
    "trap": "Opposite direction would produce hyperkalemia; aldosterone clearly increases K+ secretion.",
    "related": "Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn's) is diagnosed by aldosterone-renin ratio and treated surgically or with mineralocorticoid antagonists.",

    ],

    ],

      "EF equals cardiac output divided by heart rate"
    ],

    "difficulty": "Medium",

    "stem": "In the proximal tubule, which transporter mediates Na+-coupled glucose reabsorption at the apical membrane and what happens in the urine when plasma glucose exceeds Tm?",
    "choices": [
      "SGLT2 mediates apical Na+-glucose cotransport; exceeding transport maximum (Tm) leads to glucosuria",
      "SGLT1 exclusively at apical membrane causing no glucosuria ever",
      "GLUT2 at apical membrane actively transports glucose against gradient",
      "No transporter needed for glucose reabsorption"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "SGLT2 (and SGLT1 more distally) cotransport Na+ and glucose from lumen; filtered load exceeding Tm saturates transporters causing urinary glucose (glucosuria).",
    "trap": "GLUT transporters are facilitated carriers on basolateral membrane, not apical active transporters in proximal tubule.",
    "related": "SGLT2 inhibitors promote glucosuria to lower blood glucose in diabetes.",
    "tags": ["sodium-glucose","proximal-tubule","renal-pharmacology"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {

    "stem": "Salivon fluid from acinar cells is isotonic with plasma, but primary saliva becomes hypotonic after ductal modification. Which ductal process accounts for hypotonic final saliva?",
    "choices": [
      "Ductal reabsorption of Na+ and Cl- with limited water permeability",
      "Ductal secretion of large amounts of water",
      "Increased ductal permeability to water equaling solute",
      "No modification occurs in ducts"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "While acinar secretion is isotonic, ductal cells reabsorb Na+ and Cl- and are relatively impermeable to water, producing hypotonic final saliva; increased flow rates alter composition.",
    "trap": "Assuming ducts secrete water is incorrect; they mainly modify ionic composition.",
    "related": "Flow rate affects saliva composition: high flow more isotonic, low flow more hypotonic.",
    "tags": ["saliva","salivary-physiology","gi"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "difficulty": "Medium",

    "stem": "Somatostatin has inhibitory effects on various endocrine and exocrine functions. Which pancreatic cell secretes somatostatin and what is its primary paracrine action in the islet?",
    "choices": [
      "Delta cells secrete somatostatin which inhibits insulin and glucagon release locally",
      "Alpha cells secrete somatostatin stimulating insulin",

      "Acinar cells secrete somatostatin to digest proteins"
    ],

    "related": "Octreotide, a somatostatin analog, treats variceal bleeding and hormone-secreting tumors.",

  },

    ],

    "stem": "The spindle assembly checkpoint prevents anaphase onset until all chromosomes are properly attached. Which key event occurs when the checkpoint detects unattached kinetochores?",
    "choices": [
      "Inhibition of APC/C activation preventing securin degradation and cohesion cleavage",
      "Immediate chromosome segregation despite attachment errors",
      "Activation of cyclin-dependent kinase causing mitotic exit",
      "Increased DNA replication prior to segregation"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Unattached kinetochores activate checkpoint proteins that inhibit APC/C, preventing securin degradation and separase activation, thereby blocking cohesin cleavage and anaphase onset until proper attachment.",
    "trap": "Proceeding to anaphase with unattached chromosomes causes aneuploidy; checkpoint prevents this.",
    "related": "Checkpoint defects contribute to chromosomal instability in cancer.",

  },

    ],

      "Complete atrioventricular block preventing reentry",
      "Absence of any structural heterogeneity"

    "choices": [

      "Inhibition of surfactant production leading to immediate benefit",

    ],

    ],

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Chronic hypoxia (e.g., high altitude) increases 2,3-BPG in red blood cells. How does increased 2,3-BPG affect oxygen binding and tissue delivery?",
    "choices": [

      "Leftward shift increasing O2 affinity",
      "No effect on hemoglobin function",
      "Increases hemoglobin’s oxygen content without affecting delivery"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "2,3-BPG binds deoxyhemoglobin reducing O2 affinity (right shift) promoting oxygen unloading to tissues, an adaptive response to chronic hypoxia.",
    "trap": "Confusing 2,3-BPG with factors causing left shifts; increased 2,3-BPG enhances delivery, not binds more tightly.",
    "related": "Elevated 2,3-BPG occurs with chronic lung disease and anemia.",

    ],

    "stem": "Ryanodine receptor mutations can cause malignant hyperthermia. What is the primary abnormality in calcium handling in skeletal muscle in this condition?",
    "choices": [
      "Excessive uncontrolled Ca2+ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum leading to sustained contraction and hypermetabolism",
      "Impaired Ca2+ release causing flaccid paralysis",
      "Complete absence of Ca2+ channels",
      "Enhanced Ca2+ reuptake preventing contraction"
    ],
    "answer": 0,

    "stem": "Baroreceptors adapt to sustained pressure changes such that their firing rate resets. How does this adaptation affect blood pressure regulation in chronic hypertension?",
    "choices": [
      "Baroreflex set point shifts upward blunting reflex-mediated lowering of elevated pressure",
      "Baroreceptors permanently increase sensitivity to high pressures lowering BP",
      "No adaptation occurs so baroreceptors continuously lower BP",
      "Adaptation reverses hypertension spontaneously"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Baroreceptors adapt to sustained elevated pressures by resetting to a higher operating point, reducing their capacity to buffer chronic hypertension via reflex mechanisms.",
    "trap": "Assuming increased sensitivity would counter hypertension is incorrect; adaptation reduces reflex response.",
    "related": "Long-term BP regulation depends more on renal-volume mechanisms than baroreflex.",
    "tags": ["baroreflex","hypertension","autonomics"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [

      "Only GABA binding causing inhibition",
      "Voltage change alone without ligand causing opening",
      "Calcium binding to extracellular domain independent of depolarization"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "NMDA receptors are ligand- and voltage-gated: glutamate must bind and the postsynaptic membrane must be depolarized to expel Mg2+ blockade, allowing Ca2+ influx that triggers signaling for LTP (synaptic strengthening).",
    "trap": "GABA is inhibitory and unrelated; NMDA requires both ligand and voltage conditions enabling Hebbian plasticity.",
    "related": "NMDA receptor dysfunction implicated in excitotoxicity in stroke and neurodegeneration.",

    ],

    ],

      "CO increases dissolved O2 in plasma raising true saturation",

      "CO binds myoglobin altering signal"
    ],
    "answer": 0,

    ],

      "Enhanced SERCA activity reducing cytosolic Ca2+"
    ],

      "No change in HCO3- with PaCO2 changes",
      "Immediate full compensation to normalize pH"
    ],
    "answer": 0,

    "trap": "Confusing acute with chronic compensation magnitudes leads to misinterpretation of ABGs.",

    "topic": "Stress Response",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Acute stress triggers rapid catecholamine release and slower HPA axis activation. Which immediate autonomic output is responsible for rapid epinephrine release?",
    "choices": [
      "Preganglionic sympathetic fibers stimulate adrenal medulla chromaffin cells to release epinephrine",
      "Parasympathetic vagal stimulation causes epinephrine release",
      "HPA axis releases epinephrine immediately",
      "Thyroid hormone modulates acute epinephrine release"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Sympathetic preganglionic neurons release ACh onto chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla causing rapid catecholamine (epinephrine, norepinephrine) secretion as part of fight-or-flight response.",
    "trap": "HPA axis releases cortisol more slowly; vagal stimulation does not elicit epinephrine.",
    "related": "Epinephrine effects include tachycardia, glycogenolysis, and bronchodilation.",

  {
    "id": "PHYS0220",
    "subject": "Muscle",
    "chapter": "Skeletal Muscle",
    "topic": "Excitation Threshold",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Hyperkalemia reduces resting membrane potential negativity. How does this paradoxically lead to decreased muscle excitability at high K+ concentrations?",
    "choices": [
      "Inactivation of voltage-gated Na+ channels due to less negative resting potential reduces available channels for APs",
      "Enhanced Na+ channel activation increasing excitability",
      "Increased K+ conductance hyperpolarizes cell increasing excitability",
      "K+ has no effect on membrane excitability"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Elevated extracellular K+ makes resting membrane potential less negative, inactivating some Na+ channels and decreasing excitability; mild hyperkalemia may increase excitability, but severe hyperkalemia causes conduction block.",
    "trap": "Assuming depolarization always increases excitability ignores Na+ channel inactivation dynamics.",
    "related": "ECG changes progress with increasing K+ culminating in sine-wave and cardiac arrest.",
    "tags": ["electrolytes","muscle-excitability","clinical"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "The subendocardium is more vulnerable to ischemia than subepicardium. Which reason best explains this vulnerability?",
    "choices": [
      "Higher intramural pressure during systole compresses subendocardial vessels reducing perfusion",
      "Subendocardium has greater collateral flow",
      "Subendocardial oxygen demand is minimal",
      "Subepicardium undergoes more compression"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Systolic intramural pressure compresses subendocardial vessels more, and combined with higher wall stress and O2 demand, subendocardium is more prone to ischemia especially during tachycardia or low perfusion states.",
    "trap": "Collateral flow and oxygen demand are not protective in subendocardium.",
    "related": "Subendocardial ischemia often presents with ST depressions on ECG.",
    "tags": ["subendocardium","ischemia","cardiology"],

    "difficulty": "Hard",

    "stem": "Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) causes chronic hypercapnia. Which mechanism underlies hypoventilation in OHS?",
    "choices": [
      "Blunted central ventilatory drive plus increased work of breathing due to obesity",
      "Enhanced respiratory drive causing hypocapnia",
      "Primary muscular dystrophy of diaphragm only",
      "Excessive hyperventilation during sleep"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "OHS involves reduced central respiratory drive response to hypercapnia and hypoxia and increased work of breathing from obesity (reduced compliance), leading to chronic hypoventilation and hypercapnia.",
    "trap": "Not due to primary diaphragm disease in typical OHS.",

    "topic": "Renal Sodium Handling",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "High dietary sodium intake normally increases natriuresis. Which intrarenal mechanism increases sodium excretion in response to increased arterial pressure (pressure natriuresis)?",
    "choices": [
      "Increased renal perfusion raises GFR and reduces proximal tubular Na+ reabsorption",
      "Decreased renal blood flow increases sodium reabsorption",
      "Increased aldosterone promotes natriuresis",
      "Renal nerves cause sodium retention in response to high pressure"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Higher arterial pressure increases GFR and reduces proximal Na+ reabsorption (via decreased sympathetic tone and Starling forces), producing pressure natriuresis to restore volume balance.",
    "trap": "Aldosterone increases Na+ retention; pressure natriuresis is opposite.",
    "related": "Hypertension often involves impaired pressure-natriuresis leading to sodium retention.",
    "tags": ["pressure-natriuresis","sodium","renal"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

      "Antibiotics directly activate C. difficile toxins in lumen",

    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Broad-spectrum antibiotics perturb normal microbiota reducing competition and colonization resistance thereby permitting opportunistic overgrowth of C. difficile which produces toxins causing pseudomembranous colitis.",

    "related": "Fecal microbiota transplantation restores colonization resistance in recurrent infection.",

      "Open KATP channels reducing secretion",

    ],

      "No structural adaptation occurs",
      "Decreased blood volume to reduce workload"
    ],

    "related": "Monitoring is needed if preexisting heart disease to avoid decompensation.",

      "Peripheral nerves exclusively",

    "explanation": "Excess iron deposits in liver causing fibrosis/cirrhosis, myocardium causing restrictive/dilated cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias, and endocrine tissues causing diabetes and hypogonadism; chelation therapy mitigates risk.",

    ],

      "Increased urea enhances cross-bridge cycling"
    ],

      "Both total and forward stroke volume decrease immediately",
      "Only forward stroke volume increases while regurgitant is zero",
      "No hemodynamic changes occur"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "In chronic MR the LV ejects both forward and regurgitant volumes; total SV increases via Frank–Starling compensation, maintaining forward flow initially, but progressive dilation leads to CHF later.",

    "related": "Surgical repair considered based on symptoms and ventricular size/function.",

    "choices": [
      "Sudden increase in pulmonary vascular resistance raising RV afterload and causing dilatation and ischemia",
      "Decrease in systemic vascular resistance reducing RV load",
      "Left ventricular dilation that protects RV",
      "Pulmonary vasodilation decreasing RV pressure"
    ],

    ],

    ],

      "Low CO, low PCWP, low SVR",

    ],

      "No hormonal influence on pigmentation"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Primary adrenal failure (Addison disease) results in low cortisol with lack of negative feedback causing high ACTH; ACTH precursor POMC also yields MSH stimulating melanocytes and hyperpigmentation. Secondary causes have low ACTH.",
    "trap": "Secondary adrenal insufficiency has low ACTH so no hyperpigmentation.",
    "related": "Consider autoimmune causes and check ACTH/cortisol levels.",

    ],

    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "G6PD is the rate-limiting enzyme in the pentose phosphate pathway producing NADPH, required for maintaining reduced glutathione that detoxifies oxidants in RBCs.",
    "trap": "Glycolysis and Krebs are not primary NADPH sources for glutathione recycling in RBCs.",
    "related": "Drugs like primaquine or sulfonamides can trigger hemolytic episodes in G6PD deficiency.",
    "tags": ["g6pd","oxidative-stress","hematology"],

  },

    "choices": [

    ],

    "stem": "The prolonged refractory period in cardiac myocytes prevents tetany. Which ionic current primarily maintains the plateau phase and prolongs refractoriness?",
    "choices": [

      "Transient outward K+ current (Ito) lengthens plateau",
      "Fast Na+ current alone maintains plateau",
      "Chloride currents create the plateau"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "L-type (long-lasting) Ca2+ channels carry inward current during phase 2 balancing K+ efflux prolonging action potential and refractory period to avoid tetany in cardiac muscle.",
    "trap": "Fast Na+ is phase 0; Ito contributes to early repolarization not plateau maintenance.",

    "topic": "Capillary Recruitment",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "During exercise skeletal muscle blood flow increases via capillary recruitment. What is the physiologic advantage of capillary recruitment in active muscle?",
    "choices": [
      "Increased surface area for diffusion reduces oxygen diffusion distance and enhances exchange",
      "Decreased surface area reduces metabolite delivery",
      "Recruitment only increases vascular resistance",
      "Capillary recruitment removes erythrocytes preventing oxygen delivery"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Capillary recruitment increases perfused surface area, reduces diffusion distances to mitochondria, and improves oxygen and substrate delivery during increased metabolic demand.",
    "trap": "Opposite effects would impair exchange; recruitment lowers, not raises, resistance per tissue unit.",
    "related": "Training increases capillary density improving endurance performance.",
    "tags": ["capillary-recruitment","exercise","microcirculation"],

  },

    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "A patient with metabolic acidosis increases ventilation to compensate. According to Henderson–Hasselbalch approximations, how does acute respiratory compensation affect PaCO2 per 1 mEq/L decrease in HCO3-?",
    "choices": [
      "Decrease PaCO2 by about 1.2 mm Hg per 1 mEq/L fall in HCO3- (acute compensation is modest)",
      "Increase PaCO2 significantly with acidosis",
      "No change in PaCO2 during metabolic acidosis",
      "PaCO2 decreases by 10 mm Hg per 1 mEq/L change"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Acute ventilatory compensation is partial; empiric Winter's formula approximates expected PaCO2 ≈ 1.5 × HCO3- + 8 ± 2; thus per 1 mEq/L change small PaCO2 shifts occur (approx 1–1.5 mm Hg), not huge immediate corrections.",
    "trap": "Chronic respiratory adjustments are larger but not immediate; inverse relationships apply.",
    "related": "Use Winter formula to assess appropriate respiratory compensation.",
    "tags": ["acid-base","compensation","renal"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    ],
