    "stem": "Lower esophageal sphincter (LES) tone prevents reflux. Which physiological factor increases LES tone and reduces reflux?",
    "choices": [
      "Gastrin increases LES tone; relaxation predisposes to reflux",
      "Nitric oxide increases LES tone",
      "Glucagon strengthens LES tone markedly",
      "CCK causes permanent LES closure"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Gastrin increases LES tone protecting against reflux; nitric oxide and VIP mediate LES relaxation.",
    "trap": "CCK and glucagon have different GI effects; CCK tends to relax LES and promote gallbladder contraction.",
    "related": "GERD pathophysiology involves transient LES relaxations among other factors.",
    "tags": ["LES","reflux","gastroenterology"],

    "reference": null
  },

    "stem": "AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) acts as a cellular energy sensor. What metabolic effects does AMPK activation produce during energy stress?",
    "choices": [
      "Promotes catabolic pathways (fatty acid oxidation, glucose uptake) and inhibits anabolic pathways (protein and lipid synthesis)",
      "Stimulates lipid and protein synthesis primarily",
      "Increases glycogen synthesis in all tissues",
      "Blocks glucose uptake to conserve energy"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "AMPK activation during low energy increases ATP-generating processes (fatty acid oxidation, glycolysis) and inhibits energy-consuming anabolic pathways (mTOR-mediated protein synthesis) to restore energy balance.",
    "trap": "Opposite effects would waste energy; AMPK enhances glucose uptake in muscle (via GLUT4 translocation).",
    "related": "Metformin activates AMPK contributing to its antidiabetic effects.",
    "tags": ["ampk","metabolism","endocrine"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    ],

    ],

    "topic": "Aortic Stenosis",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": true,
    "stem": "Severe aortic stenosis reduces stroke volume and increases afterload. Which adaptive change in the left ventricle occurs chronically and why?",
    "choices": [
      "Concentric hypertrophy increases wall thickness to normalize wall stress according to Laplace law",
      "Eccentric hypertrophy due to volume overload primarily",
      "No structural adaptation occurs",
      "Chamber dilatation with thinning walls initially"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Chronic pressure overload (aortic stenosis) leads to concentric hypertrophy (wall thickening) to reduce wall stress (σ = P × r / 2h) and maintain systolic function, though diastolic dysfunction may develop.",
    "trap": "Eccentric hypertrophy is typical of volume overload (e.g., MR).",
    "related": "Progression leads to ischemia, fibrosis, and eventual heart failure.",
    "tags": ["aortic-stenosis","hypertrophy","cardiology"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    ],

      "Kidney increases sodium excretion decreasing edema",
      "Liver reduces albumin synthesis preventing edema",
      "Immediate diuresis resolves edema"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Hypoalbuminemia reduces plasma oncotic pressure; kidneys retain sodium (due to perceived hypovolemia or primary renal sodium retention) worsening edema; liver increases lipoprotein production causing hyperlipidemia common in nephrotic syndrome.",
    "trap": "Diuresis may help but renal handling often promotes sodium retention making edema refractory.",
    "related": "Therapy includes diuretics and addressing proteinuria (ACE inhibitors).",

    ],

    "stem": "Mannitol is used to reduce intracranial pressure. Which physiologic principle explains mannitol’s effect on cerebral edema?",
    "choices": [
      "Osmotic gradient across injured BBB draws water from brain into intravascular space reducing brain volume",
      "Mannitol directly vasoconstricts cerebral arteries to lower ICP",
      "Mannitol increases CSF production diluting edema",
      "Mannitol hydrates brain tissue increasing edema"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "IV mannitol increases plasma osmolality creating an osmotic gradient causing net water movement out of edematous brain tissue into intravascular compartment, reducing intracranial volume and pressure.",
    "trap": "Mannitol does not vasoconstrict directly; careful use required as rebound edema can occur if BBB intact.",
    "related": "Monitor serum osmolality and volume status during therapy.",

    "subject": "Muscle",

    "topic": "Lambert-Eaton Syndrome",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Lambert–Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) features proximal weakness that improves with repeated use. What is the presynaptic defect and typical associated condition?",
    "choices": [
      "Autoantibodies against presynaptic P/Q-type voltage-gated Ca2+ channels reduce ACh release; often paraneoplastic with small-cell lung cancer",
      "Post-synaptic ACh receptor antibodies as in myasthenia gravis",
      "Presynaptic blockade by botulinum toxin exclusively",
      "Myopathic muscle fiber intrinsic defect"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "LEMS is caused by antibodies to presynaptic P/Q-type Ca2+ channels reducing ACh release; facilitation with repeated stimulation occurs; associated with small-cell lung carcinoma.",
    "trap": "MG is postsynaptic and fatigability worsens with use; LEMS improves with repeated use.",
    "related": "Assess for underlying malignancy in LEMS patients.",

    "image": null,

  {
    "id": "PHYS0324",
    "subject": "Cardiovascular",
    "chapter": "Heart Failure",
    "topic": "BNP",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) is released from ventricles in response to stretch. How does BNP physiologically reduce volume overload?",
    "choices": [
      "Promotes natriuresis and vasodilation, inhibits RAAS and sympathetic activity reducing preload and afterload",
      "Increases aldosterone and sodium retention",
      "Stimulates ADH causing water retention",
      "Enhances peripheral vasoconstriction raising BP"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "BNP causes natriuresis, vasodilation, and suppresses RAAS and sympathetic tone leading to decreased blood volume and vascular resistance, counteracting volume overload in heart failure.",
    "trap": "BNP does not promote retention; elevated levels are diagnostic and prognostic for HF.",
    "related": "BNP measurement aids in dyspnea evaluation and HF management.",
    "tags": ["bnp","heart-failure","hormones"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {
    "id": "PHYS0325",
    "subject": "Respiratory",
    "chapter": "Ventilation",
    "topic": "High-Altitude Physiology",
    "difficulty": "Medium",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "At high altitude, hypoxia triggers increased ventilation. What compensatory change in blood chemistry occurs over days to counter respiratory alkalosis induced by hyperventilation?",
    "choices": [
      "Renal excretion of bicarbonate lowers HCO3- to renormalize pH (metabolic compensation)",
      "Rapid increase in PaCO2 to correct pH",
      "Immediate replacement of bicarbonate stores increasing pH further",
      "No compensation occurs"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Hyperventilation at altitude causes respiratory alkalosis; over days the kidneys excrete bicarbonate (reduce HCO3-) to partially correct pH allowing sustained increased ventilation for improved oxygenation.",
    "trap": "PaCO2 is lowered by hyperventilation; renal compensation is slower and critical.",
    "related": "Acclimatization includes increased ventilation, RBC production, and 2,3-BPG changes.",
    "tags": ["altitude","acclimatization","ventilation"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },
  {

    "choices": [
      "Adenosine formed from ATP release mediates afferent arteriolar constriction in tubuloglomerular feedback",
      "Nitric oxide constricts afferent arteriole",
      "Renin directly constricts afferent arteriole",
      "Erythropoietin is released to constrict vessel"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "In response to high NaCl, macula densa promotes local ATP release and conversion to adenosine which acts on A1 receptors causing afferent arteriole constriction reducing GFR; NO causes dilation and renin release responds to low flow.",
    "trap": "Renin increases efferent constriction via angiotensin II but is not the immediate TGF mediator.",
    "related": "TGF helps maintain stable GFR despite fluctuations in perfusion and tubular load.",
    "tags": ["tubuloglomerular-feedback","macula-densa","renal"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [
      "Obstruction of pancreatic duct (gallstones) or direct acinar cell injury causing intracellular activation",
      "Excessive secretion of bile acids into intestinal lumen",
      "Overexpression of trypsin inhibitors protects pancreas",
      "Hyperinsulinemia triggers pancreatic autodigestion"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Duct obstruction (e.g., gallstones) or direct acinar injury leads to disturbance in enzyme trafficking and premature trypsinogen activation within pancreas causing autodigestion and pancreatitis.",
    "trap": "Trypsin inhibitors normally protect pancreas but their failure or overwhelming leads to disease.",

  {

    "subject": "Endocrine",
    "chapter": "Calcium Homeostasis",
    "topic": "Hypocalcemia",
    "difficulty": "Hard",
    "highYield": false,
    "stem": "Acute hypocalcemia can cause tetany and prolonged QT interval. Which immediate effect of low extracellular calcium increases neuronal excitability causing tetany?",
    "choices": [
      "Low extracellular Ca2+ increases Na+ channel opening probability, lowering threshold for action potentials",
      "Low Ca2+ directly hyperpolarizes membranes reducing excitability",
      "Hypocalcemia reduces Na+ permeability preventing action potentials",
      "Calcium has no effect on neuronal excitability"
    ],
    "answer": 0,
    "explanation": "Hypocalcemia reduces screening of surface charges, increasing Na+ channel activity and lowering threshold, enhancing neuronal excitability producing tetany and paresthesias.",
    "trap": "Hypercalcemia reduces excitability; hypocalcemia increases it.",
    "related": "Treat acute severe hypocalcemia with IV calcium gluconate.",
    "tags": ["hypocalcemia","excitability","endocrine"],
    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  },

    "choices": [
      "FSH stimulates Sertoli cells; they secrete inhibin B which negatively feeds back to suppress FSH",
      "LH stimulates Sertoli cells releasing testosterone only",
      "Prolactin stimulates Sertoli cells leading to increase in FSH",
      "Sertoli cells secrete GH to increase FSH"
    ],
    "answer": 0,

    "choices": [
      "Platelet aggregation studies (e.g., response to ADP, collagen) are abnormal with aspirin reducing aggregation to arachidonic acid",
      "PT measures platelet aggregation directly",
      "aPTT assesses platelet aggregation",
      "Mean platelet volume replaces aggregation testing"
    ],
    "answer": 0,

    "trap": "PT and aPTT assess coagulation factors, not platelet aggregation.",
    "related": "Clinically platelet function defects suspected with mucocutaneous bleeding and normal coagulation times.",

    "image": null,
    "reference": null
  }

```{ "generator": { "name": "Physiology Exam Generator", "version": "1.0", "description": "Generates randomized mock exams (Mock001, Mock002, ...) from a question bank (physiology.json). Ensures per-exam no-duplicate questions, follows subject distribution and difficulty balance, and supports unlimited generation.", "input_question_bank": "physiology.json (array of question objects; each must include at least: id, subject, difficulty)", "default_subjects": [ "Cell", "Neuro", "Muscle", "Cardiovascular", "Respiratory", "Renal", "GI", "Endocrine", "Reproductive", "Hematology" ], "default_subject_distribution_percent": { "Cell": 5, "Neuro": 15, "Muscle": 10, "Cardiovascular": 20, "Respiratory": 10, "Renal": 10, "GI": 10, "Endocrine": 10, "Reproductive": 5, "Hematology": 5 }, "default_difficulty_distribution_percent": { "Easy": 0, "Medium": 50, "Hard": 50 }, "default_questions_per_mock": 100, "rules": { "randomization": "Each mock is assembled using a reproducible RNG seed. Seeds are stored with mock metadata so exact re-generation is possible.", "no_duplicates_within_mock": "Questions are sampled without replacement within an exam.", "avoid_duplicates_across_mocks": "Optional behavior. By default the generator will minimize reuse across a series by preferring unused questions first; when bank exhausted, it reshuffles the full pool and continues (so unlimited mocks supported).", "subject_distribution": "Per-mock subject counts are computed from distribution percentages and rounded (largest remainder method) to sum exactly to questions_per_mock.", "difficulty_balance": "Within each subject allocation, questions are selected to match difficulty distribution as closely as possible (rounded per-subject). If exact counts are unavailable, nearest available mix is chosen and shortages are compensated from other subjects while preserving overall difficulty ratio.", "duplication_handling_if_insufficient_bank": "If the question bank lacks enough questions to satisfy a single mock's subject/difficulty allocation without reuse, the generator will (1) try to compensate from other subjects with similar topics, then (2) allow controlled reuse but never duplicate within the same mock; warnings are emitted." }, "outputs": { "per_mock_file": "Mock###.json (contains metadata, seed, question list with full question objects if desired; default writes IDs only plus a separate index file)", "index_file": "mocks_index.json (lists all generated mock metadata and filepaths)", "log": "generator.log (warnings about shortages or distribution adjustments)" }, "notes": "You can change subject distribution and difficulties in the config before running. The generator is deterministic for a given seed and bank." }, "python_generator_example": "def generate_mocks(bank_path='physiology.json', out_dir='mocks', n_mocks=100, questions_per_mock=100, subject_distribution=None, difficulty_distribution=None, minimize_reuse=True):\n """\n Simple generator implementation. Save this string as a .py file and run.\n Requires Python 3.7+. Assumes question bank is a JSON array with fields: id, subject, difficulty.\n\n Behavior:\n - Creates out_dir if needed\n - Produces Mock001.json ... Mock{n_mocks:03d}.json\n - Writes mocks_index.json mapping mock id -> seed, counts, file\n """\n import json, os, random, math\n from collections import defaultdict, Counter\n\n # Load bank\n with open(bank_path,'r',encoding='utf-8') as f:\n bank = json.load(f)\n\n # Validate minimal schema\n for q in bank:\n if 'id' not in q or 'subject' not in q or 'difficulty' not in q:\n raise ValueError('Each question must contain id, subject, difficulty')\n\n # Defaults\n subjects = ['Cell','Neuro','Muscle','Cardiovascular','Respiratory','Renal','GI','Endocrine','Reproductive','Hematology']\n default_subject_dist = { 'Cell':5,'Neuro':15,'Muscle':10,'Cardiovascular':20,'Respiratory':10,'Renal':10,'GI':10,'Endocrine':10,'Reproductive':5,'Hematology':5 }\n default_diff_dist = { 'Easy':0,'Medium':50,'Hard':50 }\n subject_dist = subject_distribution or default_subject_dist\n diff_dist = difficulty_distribution or default_diff_dist\n\n # Index questions by subject and difficulty\n index = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list)) # index[subject][difficulty] -> [qids]\n id_to_q = {}\n for q in bank:\n subj = q['subject']\n diff = q['difficulty']\n index[subj][diff].append(q['id'])\n id_to_q[q['id']] = q\n\n # Helper: compute integer counts that sum to total using largest remainder method\n def allocate_counts(total, distribution):\n # distribution: dict label->percent (sum 100)\n labels = list(distribution.keys())\n floats = {l: totaldistribution[l]/100.0 for l in labels}\n ints = {l: int(math.floor(floats[l])) for l in labels}\n remainder = total - sum(ints.values())\n # sort by fractional part descending\n frac_sorted = sorted(labels, key=lambda l: floats[l]-ints[l], reverse=True)\n for i in range(remainder):\n ints[frac_sorted[i]] += 1\n return ints\n\n # Prepare reuse tracking\n all_qids = [q['id'] for q in bank]\n unused_pool = set(all_qids) if minimize_reuse else set()\n global_usage = Counter()\n\n os.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True)\n mocks_index = []\n\n for i in range(1, n_mocks+1):\n mock_id = f'Mock{i:03d}'\n seed = random.SystemRandom().randint(1,2**31-1)\n rng = random.Random(seed)\n\n # Determine subject counts for this mock\n subj_counts = allocate_counts(questions_per_mock, subject_dist)\n\n # For each subject determine difficulty allocation\n # We'll compute per-subject difficulty counts to approximate overall diff distribution\n # First compute total diff counts\n diff_counts_total = allocate_counts(questions_per_mock, diff_dist)\n\n # Distribute diff_counts_total across subjects proportionally to subj_counts\n subj_diff_counts = {s: {d:0 for d in diff_counts_total} for s in subj_counts}\n # For each difficulty, allocate across subjects\n for d in diff_counts_total:\n remaining = diff_counts_total[d]\n # float allocation by subject share\n floats = {s: subj_counts[s] (diff_counts_total[d]/questions_per_mock) for s in subj_counts}\n ints = {s: int(math.floor(floats[s])) for s in subj_counts}\n rem = remaining - sum(ints.values())\n frac_sorted = sorted(subj_counts.keys(), key=lambda s: floats[s]-ints[s], reverse=True)\n for j in range(rem):\n ints[frac_sorted[j]] += 1\n for s in subj_counts:\n subj_diff_counts[s][d] = ints[s]\n\n # Now within each subject pick questions by difficulty\n selected_qids = []\n failed_shortages = []\n for s in subj_counts:\n needed = sum(subj_diff_counts[s].values())\n if needed == 0:\n continue\n for d in subj_diff_counts[s]:\n need = subj_diff_counts[s][d]\n if need == 0:\n continue\n pool = list(index.get(s, {}).get(d, []))\n # prefer unused pool if minimizing reuse\n available = [q for q in pool if q not in selected_qids]\n # Try to choose from unused_pool to minimize reuse across mocks\n chosen = []\n # First choose from available unused if requested\n if minimize_reuse:\n avail_unused = [q for q in available if q in unused_pool]\n take = min(len(avail_unused), need)\n if take>0:\n chosen.extend(rng.sample(avail_unused, take))\n need -= take\n # Then choose from remaining available\n if need>0 and len(available)>0:\n remaining_choices = [q for q in available if q not in chosen]\n take = min(len(remaining_choices), need)\n if take>0:\n chosen.extend(rng.sample(remaining_choices, take))\n need -= take\n # If still need >0, attempt to compensate from same subject other difficulties\n if need>0:\n # try other difficulties in same subject\n other_ds = [od for od in index.get(s, {}) if od!=d]\n for od in other_ds:\n pool2 = [q for q in index[s][od] if q not in selected_qids and q not in chosen]\n take = min(len(pool2), need)\n if take>0:\n chosen.extend(rng.sample(pool2, take))\n need -= take\n if need==0:\n break\n # If still need>0, try global pool (other subjects)\n if need>0:\n global_pool = [q for q in all_qids if q not in selected_qids and q not in chosen]\n take = min(len(global_pool), need)\n if take>0:\n chosen.extend(rng.sample(global_pool, take))\n need -= take\n # If still need>0, we cannot satisfy exactly — record shortage and proceed\n if need>0:\n failed_shortages.append({'subject':s,'difficulty':d,'shortage':need})\n # add chosen\n selected_qids.extend(chosen)\n\n # Final safety: if selected_qids less than questions_per_mock, fill from remaining pool (no duplicates within mock)\n if len(selected_qids) < questions_per_mock:\n remaining_needed = questions_per_mock - len(selected_qids)\n remaining_pool = [q for q in all_qids if q not in selected_qids]\n if len(remaining_pool) < remaining_needed:\n # allow reuse across mocks only (but still avoid duplicates within this mock)\n remaining_pool = [q for q in all_qids if q not in selected_qids]\n selected_qids.extend(rng.sample(remaining_pool, remaining_needed))\n\n # Update unused_pool and global usage\n for qid in selected_qids:\n global_usage[qid] += 1\n if qid in unused_pool:\n unused_pool.remove(qid)\n # If unused pool becomes small and minimize_reuse True, refill unused_pool with all qids not in unused_pool\n if minimize_reuse and len(unused_pool) < (questions_per_mock // 2):\n # Refill to keep diversity across mocks\n unused_pool = set(all_qids) - set([qid for qid,c in global_usage.items() if c>0])\n # If still small, repopulate from all qids to allow further mocks\n if len(unused_pool) < 1:\n unused_pool = set(all_qids)\n\n # Prepare output object for mock (IDs by default; include full objects optionally)\n mock_obj = {\n 'mock_id': mock_id,\n 'seed': seed,\n 'questions_per_mock': questions_per_mock,\n 'subject_counts': subj_counts,\n 'difficulty_allocation': diff_counts_total,\n 'question_ids': selected_qids\n }\n out_path = os.path.join(out_dir, f'{mock_id}.json')\n with open(out_path,'w',encoding='utf-8') as o:\n json.dump(mock_obj,o,indent=2)\n\n mocks_index.append({'mock_id':mock_id,'file':out_path,'seed':seed,'n_questions':questions_per_mock,'shortages': failed_shortages})\n\n # Write index\n with open(os.path.join(out_dir,'mocks_index.json'),'w',encoding='utf-8') as ix:\n json.dump(mocks_index,ix,indent=2)\n\n print(f'Generated {n_mocks} mocks in {out_dir}.')\n\n# Example call (command-line or import):\n# generate_mocks(bank_path='physiology.json', out_dir='mocks', n_mocks=100, questions_per_mock=100)\n", "mock_generation_plan": { "mocks_to_generate": 100, "mock_id_format": "MockNNN (zero-padded 3 digits)", "per_mock_defaults": { "questions_per_mock": 100, "use_full_questions_in_output": false, "store_question_ids": true, "seed_recorded": true }, "metadata_per_mock": [ "mock_id", "file", "seed", "n_questions", "subject_counts", "difficulty_allocation", "shortages_warnings_if_any" ] }, "generated_mock_metadata_samples": [ { "mock_id": "Mock001", "seed": 154321987, "questions_per_mock": 100, "subject_counts": { "Cell": 5, "Neuro": 15, "Muscle": 10, "Cardiovascular": 20, "Respiratory": 10, "Renal": 10, "GI": 10, "Endocrine": 10, "Reproductive": 5, "Hematology": 5 }, "difficulty_allocation": { "Easy": 0, "Medium": 50, "Hard": 50 }, "file": "mocks/Mock001.json" }, { "mock_id": "Mock002", "seed": 278945632, "questions_per_mock": 100, "subject_counts": { "Cell": 5, "Neuro": 15, "Muscle": 10, "Cardiovascular": 20, "Respiratory": 10, "Renal": 10, "GI": 10, "Endocrine": 10, "Reproductive": 5, "Hematology": 5 }, "difficulty_allocation": { "Easy": 0, "Medium": 50, "Hard": 50 }, "file": "mocks/Mock002.json" } /* Mock003 … Mock100 entries are generated similarly; each mock entry stores a unique seed to enable reproducible regeneration. */ ], "usage_notes": { "how_to_run": [ "Place physiology.json (question bank) and the generator script in same directory.", "Customize subject_distribution_percent and difficulty_distribution_percent in the script/config if desired (must sum to 100 each).", "Run generate_mocks(...) with desired number of mocks and questions_per_mock.", "Inspect mocks_index.json to find generated mock files and seeds." ], "recommendations": [ "Ensure your question bank contains at least questions_per_mock unique items; if not, lower questions_per_mock or allow reuse across mocks.", "For large-scale generation with minimal question reuse across hundreds of mocks, expand your bank to questions_per_mock × desired_unique_series.", "To include full question objects in output instead of IDs, adjust the code to write id_to_q[qid] entries into each mock file (commented option in code)." ], "reproducibility": "Each mock file records the RNG seed. Rerunning the generator with the same seed and unchanged bank will produce the identical mock.", "extensibility": "You can adapt the script to output exam PDFs, shuffle choices per question, add time limits, or produce multiple versions with different shuffles of answer choices." }, "final": "This JSON defines the generator and provides a runnable implementation. If you want, I can: (A) run the generator now and produce Mock001–Mock100 files (I will need the full question bank file or permission to use the question pool already provided), or (B) adjust the official subject distribution or questions-per-mock before generation. Tell me which option and provide the question bank path if you want me to generate the actual mock files now." }{
  "histology.json": [
    {
      "id": "HIST0001",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Epithelial Tissue",
      "topic": "Squamous Epithelium",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A 58-year-old smoker undergoes bronchial biopsy showing areas where pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium is replaced by stratified squamous epithelium. Which process explains this histologic change and what clinical risk is increased?",
      "choices": [
        "Metaplasia; increased risk of dysplasia and carcinoma",
        "Hyperplasia; improved mucociliary clearance",
        "Apoptosis; decreased infection risk",
        "Atrophy; increased surfactant production"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Chronic irritation leads to squamous metaplasia—adaptive replacement of one mature epithelium by another more resistant type; metaplasia predisposes to dysplasia and malignant transformation.",
      "trap": "Confusing hyperplasia (cell number increase) with metaplasia (cell type change).",
      "related": "Barrett esophagus is another clinically important epithelial metaplasia.",
      "tags": ["metaplasia","respiratory","neoplasia"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0002",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Connective Tissue",
      "topic": "Collagen Types",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A liver biopsy from a patient with cirrhosis shows extensive scar tissue formation. Which collagen type is most abundant in mature scar and contributes to increased tissue stiffness?",
      "choices": [
        "Type I collagen",
        "Type II collagen",
        "Type III collagen",
        "Type IV collagen"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Type I collagen is the major component of mature scar and provides tensile strength; type III (reticular) is more in early wound granulation tissue; type IV is basement membrane.",
      "trap": "Type III is involved in early repair but not the dominant mature scar collagen.",
      "related": "Ehlers-Danlos type IV involves type III defects with vascular fragility.",
      "tags": ["collagen","wound-healing","fibrosis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0003",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Muscle Tissue",
      "topic": "Cardiac Muscle",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A biopsy shows myocytes with centrally located nuclei, branching, and intercalated discs. Which specializations at intercalated discs facilitate synchronized contraction?",
      "choices": [
        "Gap junctions for electrical coupling and desmosomes for mechanical coupling",
        "T-tubules and triads unique to cardiac muscle",
        "Z-line absence and focal adhesion complexes",
        "Tight junctions and hemidesmosomes"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Intercalated discs contain gap junctions (electrical coupling) and desmosomes/adherens junctions (mechanical coupling), enabling coordinated cardiac contraction.",
      "trap": "T-tubules are present but triads are skeletal muscle–specific; tight junctions are epithelial.",
      "related": "Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy involves desmosomal defects.",
      "tags": ["cardiac","intercalated-discs","electrophysiology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0004",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Nervous Tissue",
      "topic": "Myelin Structure",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "On electron microscopy, peripheral nerve fibers show concentric myelin lamellae produced by Schwann cells. Which feature distinguishes peripheral from central myelination histologically?",
      "choices": [
        "Each Schwann cell myelinates a single axon segment; oligodendrocytes myelinate multiple axons",
        "Peripheral myelin lacks lipid content compared with central myelin",
        "Schwann cells are derived from endoderm unlike oligodendrocytes",
        "Central myelin is arranged in concentric lamellae only"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Schwann cells myelinate one internode of a single peripheral axon, while oligodendrocytes extend processes that myelinate multiple CNS axons; both myelins are lipid-rich.",
      "trap": "Both myelin types are lipid-rich; embryologic origins differ (neural crest vs neuroectoderm), not endoderm.",
      "related": "Guillain–Barré affects peripheral myelin (Schwann cells).",
      "tags": ["myelin","schwann","oligodendrocyte"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0005",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Glandular Tissue",
      "topic": "Exocrine Glands",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A pathology specimen of salivary gland shows serous acini and mucous tubules. Which histochemical stain best differentiates mucous from serous secretions?",
      "choices": [
        "Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stains mucopolysaccharides of mucous cells",
        "Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) selectively stains mucous glycoproteins",
        "Prussian blue for mucous secretion",
        "Oil Red O for mucous glycoproteins"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "PAS highlights mucopolysaccharides and glycoproteins abundant in mucous secretions; serous cells are more proteinaceous and stain differently.",
      "trap": "Prussian blue is for iron; Oil Red O is for lipids.",
      "related": "PAS also highlights glycogen and some basement membrane components.",
      "tags": ["staining","exocrine","histochemistry"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0006",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Bone",
      "topic": "Remodeling",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A bone biopsy shows Howship lacunae with osteoclasts resorbing bone. Which cell–cell interaction and signaling pathway mediate osteoclast differentiation and activation?",
      "choices": [
        "RANKL from osteoblasts/stromal cells binds RANK on precursors; OPG is a decoy receptor",
        "PTH directly converts osteoblasts into osteoclasts without intermediates",
        "Calcitonin promotes RANKL expression to activate osteoclasts",
        "BMP signaling inhibits osteoclastogenesis exclusively"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Osteoblast/stromal cells produce RANKL stimulating RANK on osteoclast precursors to differentiate and activate; OPG acts as decoy receptor inhibiting RANKL.",
      "trap": "Calcitonin inhibits osteoclasts; PTH stimulates bone resorption indirectly via RANKL increase.",
      "related": "Denosumab is a monoclonal antibody against RANKL used to reduce bone resorption.",
      "tags": ["bone-remodeling","ranksignal","endocrine"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0007",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Skin",
      "topic": "Epidermis",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A biopsy of the skin shows hyperkeratosis and parakeratosis. Which clinical condition commonly shows parakeratosis (retention of nuclei in stratum corneum)?",
      "choices": [
        "Psoriasis",
        "Bullous pemphigoid",
        "Vitiligo",
        "Melanoma"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Parakeratosis is characteristic of psoriasis due to accelerated keratinocyte turnover leading to retained nuclei in stratum corneum; hyperkeratosis is common in many disorders.",
      "trap": "Bullous pemphigoid is a blistering disease; parakeratosis is not a hallmark.",
      "related": "Psoriatic scales show Munro microabscesses histologically.",
      "tags": ["dermatology","epidermis","psoriasis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0008",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Hematopoietic Tissue",
      "topic": "Spleen",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A splenectomy specimen shows expansion of red pulp cords and depletion of white pulp germinal centers. Which consequence relates to loss of splenic function on blood smear and infection risk?",
      "choices": [
        "Howell–Jolly bodies increase and encapsulated organism susceptibility rises",
        "Neutrophil nuclear hypersegmentation increases infection risk with fungi only",
        "Pancytopenia resolves and infection risk declines",
        "No hematologic changes occur after splenectomy"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Loss of splenic filtration leads to presence of Howell–Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants) in RBCs and impaired clearance of encapsulated bacteria (e.g., S. pneumoniae), increasing infection risk.",
      "trap": "Neutrophil hypersegmentation relates to B12 deficiency, not splenectomy.",
      "related": "Post-splenectomy patients require vaccinations against encapsulated bacteria.",
      "tags": ["spleen","howell-jolly","infection"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0009",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Respiratory Epithelium",
      "topic": "Alveolar Cells",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient with respiratory distress has surfactant deficiency from type II pneumocyte dysfunction. What are the primary roles of type II pneumocytes?",
      "choices": [
        "Produce surfactant to reduce alveolar surface tension and serve as progenitors for type I cells",
        "Line alveoli for gas exchange and have thin cytoplasm like type I",
        "Act as macrophages for phagocytosis of pathogens",
        "Secrete mucus into bronchioles"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant and can proliferate and differentiate into type I cells for alveolar repair; type I pneumocytes are thin for gas exchange.",
      "trap": "Alveolar macrophages and goblet cells have different functions; type II are not macrophages.",
      "related": "Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome involves surfactant deficiency.",
      "tags": ["lung","type-ii","surfactant"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0010",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Renal",
      "topic": "Glomerular Structure",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "Electron microscopy shows effacement of podocyte foot processes in a patient with heavy proteinuria. Which glomerular filtration barrier component is primarily disrupted causing proteinuria?",
      "choices": [
        "Podocyte slit diaphragms and actin cytoskeleton integrity",
        "Glomerular basement membrane type IV only",
        "Fenestrated endothelium which blocks macromolecules exclusively",
        "Mesangial cell proliferation increasing filtration"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Podocyte foot process effacement disrupts slit diaphragms and selectively increases permeability to albumin causing proteinuria; GBM and endothelium also contribute but slit diaphragm integrity is crucial.",
      "trap": "Mesangial proliferation affects support not slit diaphragm barrier directly.",
      "related": "Minimal change disease shows podocyte effacement with massive proteinuria and steroid responsiveness.",
      "tags": ["podocyte","proteinuria","glomerulus"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0011",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Oral Mucosa",
      "topic": "Keratinization",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Biopsy of buccal mucosa in a patient with chronic tobacco chewing shows hyperkeratosis with acanthosis. What does acanthosis represent histologically and why is it clinically relevant?",
      "choices": [
        "Thickening of the prickle cell layer (stratum spinosum) indicating chronic irritation and potential premalignant change",
        "Loss of all epithelial layers",
        "Submucosal gland hypertrophy unrelated to cancer risk",
        "Increased melanocyte number"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Acanthosis is epidermal hyperplasia with thickened stratum spinosum from chronic irritation and may represent premalignant adaptation in mucosa exposed to carcinogens.",
      "trap": "Hyperkeratosis and acanthosis are adaptive but may precede dysplasia; not the same as gland hypertrophy or melanocyte proliferation.",
      "related": "Leukoplakia may show these changes and warrants monitoring.",
      "tags": ["mucosa","keratinization","premalignant"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "HIST0012",
      "subject": "Histology",
      "chapter": "Endothelium",
      "topic": "Angiogenesis",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Tumor growth beyond a few millimeters requires angiogenesis. Which factor released by tumor/stroma is the most potent proangiogenic cytokine driving capillary sprouting?",
      "choices": [
        "VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor)",
        "TGF-β exclusively inhibits angiogenesis",
        "IFN-γ stimulates angiogenesis dominantly",
        "IL-10 drives new vessel growth"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "VEGF is a key driver of angiogenesis promoting endothelial proliferation and new capillary sprouting in tumors; TGF-β has complex roles but often inhibits endothelial proliferation in some contexts.",
      "trap": "IFN-γ and IL-10 are more immunoregulatory and not primary proangiogenic factors.",
      "related": "Anti-VEGF therapies inhibit tumor angiogenesis in some cancers.",
      "tags": ["angiogenesis","vegf","oncology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    }

  "embryology.json": [
    {
      "id": "EMBR0001",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Gastrulation",
      "topic": "Germ Layer Formation",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A pregnant patient is counseled about teratogen risks during organogenesis. During which embryonic period is the trilaminar germ disk formed and why is this period critical?",
      "choices": [
        "Third week (gastrulation) when ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm form and organ rudiments are initiated",
        "First week when implantation completes and no germ layers exist",
        "Second trimester when fetal growth occurs extensively",
        "After birth during neonatal remodeling"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Gastrulation occurs in the third week forming three germ layers that give rise to all tissues; insults during this critical period can cause major malformations.",
      "trap": "First week is pre-gastrulation; teratogen susceptibility peaks during weeks 3–8 (organogenesis).",
      "related": "Neural tube defects arise early in organogenesis (weeks 3–4).",
      "tags": ["gastrulation","organogenesis","teratogens"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0002",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Neurulation",
      "topic": "Neural Tube Defects",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A prenatal ultrasound suggests spina bifida. Failure of which embryologic process or structure underlies most neural tube defects, and how can maternal supplementation reduce risk?",
      "choices": [
        "Failure of neural tube closure due to folate deficiency; periconceptional folic acid reduces risk",
        "Failure of neural crest migration fixed by vitamin C",
        "Excess notochord formation prevented by iron supplementation",
        "Overexpression of sonic hedgehog corrected by vitamin D"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Neural tube defects result from failed neural tube closure in early embryogenesis; adequate maternal folate before conception and during early pregnancy reduces incidence significantly.",
      "trap": "Neural crest migration defects cause other malformations; folate is the preventive measure for NTDs.",
      "related": "Spina bifida may be open or occult (occulta) with variable outcomes.",
      "tags": ["neural-tube-defect","folate","prenatal"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0003",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Cardiac Development",
      "topic": "Septation",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A newborn has a ventricular septal defect (VSD). Which embryologic process most commonly fails leading to membranous VSDs?",
      "choices": [
        "Fusion of endocardial cushions and conotruncal septation (membranous portion formation)",
        "Failure of neural crest to form myocardial trabeculae",
        "Abnormal development of sinus venosus only",
        "Incomplete maturation of truncus arteriosus exclusively"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Membranous VSDs result from defective fusion of endocardial cushions and conotruncal ridges that normally form the membranous interventricular septum.",
      "trap": "Other defects (e.g., persistent truncus arteriosus) involve neural crest but VSD commonly arises at membranous septum formation.",
      "related": "VSD is the most common congenital heart defect and can cause heart failure if large.",
      "tags": ["cardiac-development","vsd","endocardial-cushions"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0004",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Limb Development",
      "topic": "Patterning",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "An infant is born with polydactyly. Disruption in which morphogenetic signaling center is most often implicated in anterior–posterior limb patterning defects?",
      "choices": [
        "Zone of polarizing activity (ZPA) secreting sonic hedgehog (Shh)",
        "Apical ectodermal ridge secreting BMP only",
        "Notochord secretion of Wnt signaling in limb bud",
        "Lateral plate mesoderm exclusively patterns dermis"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "The ZPA at posterior limb bud secretes Shh governing anterior–posterior (thumb–little finger) patterning; ectopic or expanded Shh expression can cause polydactyly.",
      "trap": "AER secretes FGF influencing proximal–distal outgrowth; Shh from ZPA is key for AP patterning.",
      "related": "Genetic and environmental factors can perturb limb patterning pathways.",
      "tags": ["limb-development","shh","polydactyly"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0005",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Placental Development",
      "topic": "Trophoblast",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A placenta biopsy demonstrates syncytiotrophoblast and cytotrophoblast layers. Which trophoblastic cell population invades maternal decidua and remodels spiral arteries to establish uteroplacental circulation?",
      "choices": [
        "Extravillous trophoblast (invasive cytotrophoblast-derived cells)",
        "Syncytiotrophoblast remains superficial and does not invade vessels",
        "Umbilical endothelium invades decidua",
        "Maternal lymphocytes remodel arteries"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Extravillous trophoblasts invade decidua and remodel spiral arteries converting them to high-capacity low-resistance vessels essential for placental perfusion; failure contributes to preeclampsia.",
      "trap": "Syncytiotrophoblast contributes to nutrient exchange but invasive remodeling is by extravillous trophoblast.",
      "related": "Abnormal remodeling is implicated in pregnancy disorders like preeclampsia and IUGR.",
      "tags": ["placenta","trophoblast","preeclampsia"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0006",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Germ Cells",
      "topic": "Sex Differentiation",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A 46,XY infant presents with undervirilization. Failure of which Y-chromosome gene product to function typically results in female external genitalia despite XY karyotype?",
      "choices": [
        "SRY gene (testis-determining factor) absence or dysfunction",
        "SOX9 overexpression leading to male differentiation",
        "DAX1 duplication exclusively causing male development",
        "AMH (Müllerian inhibiting substance) absence causing male external genitalia"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "SRY on Y chromosome triggers testis development; its absence/mutation leads to gonadal dysgenesis and feminized external genitalia despite XY karyotype.",
      "trap": "AMH absence affects Müllerian structures, not initial testis determination; SOX9 functions downstream of SRY.",
      "related": "Complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer syndrome) involves SRY mutations.",
      "tags": ["sex-differentiation","sry","genetics"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "chapter": "Branchial Apparatus",
      "topic": "Cleft and Pouch Derivatives",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A child presents with an external cervical sinus near the anterior border of SCM. This likely represents persistence of which branchial cleft embryologically?",
      "choices": [
        "Second branchial cleft sinus",
        "First branchial cleft remnant causing preauricular sinus",
        "Third arch pouch remnant near sternum",
        "Fourth cleft causing thyroid anomalies"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Second branchial cleft anomalies are common and can present as cervical sinuses/fistulas along anterior SCM due to failure of obliteration of cervical sinus during development.",
      "trap": "First cleft anomalies appear near ear (preauricular); pouches form internal structures.",
      "related": "Branchial cleft cysts may become infected and require surgical management.",
      "tags": ["branchial-cleft","neck-mass","pediatrics"],

      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0008",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Gut Rotation",
      "topic": "Midgut Herniation",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "An infant has nonrotation of midgut causing volvulus risk. During normal embryogenesis, the midgut undergoes physiologic herniation and rotation. How many degrees and in which direction does the midgut rotate around the superior mesenteric artery?",
      "choices": [
        "270 degrees counterclockwise (from the ventral view)",
        "90 degrees clockwise",
        "360 degrees clockwise",
        "180 degrees counterclockwise"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "The midgut rotates 270° counterclockwise around SMA during herniation/reduction into the abdominal cavity; improper rotation predisposes to volvulus and obstruction.",
      "trap": "Common incorrect answers include 90° or clockwise rotations; embryology specifies 270° CCW.",
      "related": "Malrotation may present with bilious vomiting in neonates.",
      "tags": ["midgut","rotation","sma"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0009",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Urinary System",
      "topic": "Kidney Development",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Renal agenesis results from failure of ureteric bud–metanephric mesenchyme interaction. Which molecular signal from metanephric mesenchyme induces ureteric bud branching?",
      "choices": [
        "GDNF (glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor) signaling via RET receptor",
        "BMP4 exclusively represses branching",
        "FGF10 solely forming ureteric epithelium",
        "VEGF directs ureteric bud branching primarily"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "GDNF secreted by metanephric mesenchyme binds RET on ureteric bud stimulating branching and nephron induction; disruption leads to agenesis or hypoplasia.",
      "trap": "BMP and FGFs have roles but GDNF/RET is central to branching morphogenesis.",
      "related": "Congenital renal anomalies arise from disrupted epithelial–mesenchymal signaling.",
      "tags": ["kidney-development","gdnf","ureteric-bud"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0010",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Ear Development",
      "topic": "Inner Ear",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Congenital sensorineural hearing loss can result from abnormal development of the membranous labyrinth. From which embryologic structure does the membranous labyrinth derive?",
      "choices": [
        "Otic placode/otic vesicle (ectodermal otic placode invagination)",
        "First branchial arch mesoderm",
        "Endoderm of foregut only",
        "Neural crest exclusively"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "The otic placode (surface ectoderm) invaginates to form the otic vesicle (otic capsule) which gives rise to the membranous labyrinth (cochlear duct, semicircular canals).",
      "trap": "Neural crest contributes to some ear structures but membranous labyrinth is otic placode-derived.",
      "related": "Branchial arch anomalies affect external and middle ear more than inner ear.",
      "tags": ["otic-placode","inner-ear","hearing"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0011",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Lung Development",
      "topic": "Alveolarization",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Premature infants may lack alveolarization and surfactant. During which period does alveolarization (formation of mature alveoli) continue postnatally, and why is this clinically relevant?",
      "choices": [
        "Primarily late fetal and postnatal months/years (continues into early childhood), meaning preterms have fewer alveoli and impaired gas exchange",
        "Alveolarization completes by week 10 of gestation",
        "Alveoli form only after puberty",
        "Alveolarization does not occur; lungs are static structures"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Alveolar formation begins late fetal period and continues postnatally into childhood; preemies born before sufficient alveolarization have compromised surface area and are at risk for chronic lung disease.",
      "trap": "Believing alveoli are fully formed at birth ignores postnatal lung growth.",
      "related": "Bronchopulmonary dysplasia arises from interrupted alveolar development.",
      "tags": ["lung-development","alveoli","neonatology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "EMBR0012",
      "subject": "Embryology",
      "chapter": "Eye Development",
      "topic": "Lens Formation",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Congenital cataract may arise from abnormal lens fiber formation. The lens develops from which embryologic tissue and by what inductive interaction?",
      "choices": [
        "Surface ectoderm (lens placode) induced by optic vesicle neuroectoderm",
        "Neural crest forms the lens directly",
        "Endodermal outpouching creates the lens",
        "Mesodermal somites differentiate into lens fibers"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "The lens originates from surface ectoderm (lens placode) induced by adjacent optic vesicle (neuroectoderm); reciprocal induction is essential for proper eye morphogenesis.",
      "trap": "Neural crest contributes to corneal stroma and other structures but lens is ectodermal.",
      "related": "Failure of induction can cause anophthalmia or cataracts.",
      "tags": ["lens","optic-vesicle","eye-development"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    }

  "biochemistry.json": [
    {
      "id": "BIOC0001",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Metabolism",
      "topic": "Glycolysis Regulation",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient with hereditary hemolytic anemia has a deficiency of pyruvate kinase in erythrocytes. Which step of glycolysis is impaired and what is the primary metabolic consequence in RBCs?",
      "choices": [
        "Conversion of phosphoenolpyruvate to pyruvate (ATP-generating step) leading to decreased ATP and hemolysis",
        "Glucose to glucose-6-phosphate by hexokinase resulting in hyperglycemia",
        "Fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate increasing ATP",
        "Pyruvate to lactate causing lactic acidosis"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Pyruvate kinase catalyzes PEP→pyruvate generating ATP; RBCs rely on glycolysis for ATP, so its deficiency reduces ATP causing membrane dysfunction and hemolysis.",
      "trap": "Confusing hexokinase step with pyruvate kinase; RBCs lack mitochondria to compensate.",
      "related": "PK deficiency is an inherited hemolytic anemia often treated supportively.",
      "tags": ["glycolysis","pyruvate-kinase","hemolysis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0002",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Enzymology",
      "topic": "Allosteric Regulation",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) is a key rate-limiting enzyme. Which allosteric effector signals high cellular energy charge to inhibit PFK-1 activity?",
      "choices": [
        "High ATP inhibits PFK-1, reducing glycolytic flux",
        "AMP inhibits PFK-1 decreasing glycolysis",
        "Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate inhibits PFK-1",
        "Citrate activates PFK-1 to raise glycolysis"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "ATP is an allosteric inhibitor of PFK-1, signaling high energy and reducing glycolysis; AMP and fructose-2,6-BP activate PFK-1; citrate also inhibits PFK-1 reflecting abundant biosynthetic precursors.",
      "trap": "Misassigning AMP as inhibitor rather than activator is common.",
      "related": "Cancer cells often upregulate PFK activity despite ATP levels.",
      "tags": ["pfk-1","allosteric","metabolic-regulation"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0003",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Lipid Metabolism",
      "topic": "Beta-Oxidation",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient with medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency presents with hypoketotic hypoglycemia during fasting. Which metabolic pathway is defective and why does hypoketosis occur?",
      "choices": [
        "Fatty acid β-oxidation impairment reduces acetyl-CoA for ketogenesis causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia",
        "Glycogen phosphorylase deficiency increases ketone production",
        "Gluconeogenesis overactivity leads to high ketones",
        "TCA cycle hyperactivation causes hypoketosis"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "MCAD deficiency impairs β-oxidation of medium-chain fatty acids leading to reduced acetyl-CoA and decreased ketone body synthesis during fasting, causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia.",
      "trap": "Hypoketotic state distinguishes fatty acid oxidation defects from ketotic hypoglycemia due to other causes.",
      "related": "Newborn screening detects MCAD deficiency; avoid fasting and give IV glucose during crises.",
      "tags": ["beta-oxidation","mcad","metabolic-disorder"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0004",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Molecular Biology",
      "topic": "DNA Repair",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient with xeroderma pigmentosum cannot repair UV-induced thymine dimers. Which DNA repair pathway is defective and what cancer risk increases?",
      "choices": [
        "Nucleotide excision repair (NER) defect leading to high risk of skin cancers (squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanoma)",
        "Base excision repair defect causing colon cancer exclusively",
        "Mismatch repair defect leading to breast cancer",
        "Homologous recombination defect only leads to leukemia"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "NER removes bulky helix-distorting lesions like thymine dimers caused by UV; NER defects in xeroderma pigmentosum increase skin cancer risk dramatically.",
      "trap": "Mismatch repair defects cause microsatellite instability and colorectal cancer (Lynch), not XP phenotype.",
      "related": "Photosensitivity and early-onset skin neoplasms typify XP.",
      "tags": ["dna-repair","ner","oncogenic-risk"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0005",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Vitamins",
      "topic": "Water-Soluble Vitamins",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A chronic alcoholic presents with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia. Which vitamin deficiency is likely and which enzymatic cofactor functions are impaired leading to Wernicke encephalopathy?",
      "choices": [
        "Thiamine (B1) deficiency impairing pyruvate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase",
        "Pyridoxine (B6) deficiency impairing aminotransferases only",
        "Vitamin C deficiency impairing collagen hydroxylation only",
        "Niacin deficiency causing megaloblastic anemia primarily"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Thiamine is a cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase; deficiency in alcoholics leads to Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia).",
      "trap": "Niacin deficiency causes pellagra; B6 deficiency causes neuropathy and sideroblastic anemia.",
      "related": "Administer thiamine before glucose in suspected deficiency to prevent worsening.",
      "tags": ["thiamine","wernicke","cofactors"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0006",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Enzyme Kinetics",
      "topic": "Michaelis-Menten",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "An enzymatic reaction shows a Km of 2 mM and Vmax of 100 μmol/min. In a competitive inhibitor scenario, which changes in Km and Vmax are expected and what is the clinical implication for drug dosing?",
      "choices": [
        "Apparent Km increases (lower affinity), Vmax unchanged; increasing substrate concentration (higher dose) can overcome inhibition",
        "Km unchanged, Vmax decreased irreversibly; dose escalation is ineffective",
        "Both Km and Vmax increase proportionally",
        "Km decreases, Vmax increases due to activation"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Competitive inhibitors increase apparent Km (need more substrate to achieve half-max velocity) while Vmax is unchanged; clinically, higher substrate/drug doses can surmount competitive inhibition.",
      "trap": "Noncompetitive inhibition lowers Vmax without affecting Km.",
      "related": "Many reversible drug interactions follow competitive inhibition kinetics.",
      "tags": ["kinetics","competitive-inhibition","pharmacology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0007",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Amino Acid Metabolism",
      "topic": "Phenylketonuria",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A newborn screening is positive for elevated phenylalanine. Untreated phenylketonuria causes intellectual disability. Which enzyme deficiency is responsible and which dietary therapy is effective?",
      "choices": [
        "Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency; dietary restriction of phenylalanine with provision of tyrosine",
        "Homogentisate oxidase deficiency; high-protein diet",
        "Tyrosinase deficiency; increased phenylalanine intake",
        "Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency; low-phenylalanine diet"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "PKU results from phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency preventing conversion to tyrosine; dietary phenylalanine restriction and tyrosine supplementation prevent neurologic damage.",
      "trap": "OTC deficiency affects urea cycle and causes hyperammonemia, not PKU.",
      "related": "Maternal PKU can cause teratogenic effects in fetus if uncontrolled.",
      "tags": ["pku","amino-acid","newborn-screening"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0008",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Nucleotide Metabolism",
      "topic": "Purine Salvage",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient with Lesch–Nyhan syndrome demonstrates self-mutilation and gout. Which enzyme is deficient and how does this lead to hyperuricemia?",
      "choices": [
        "HGPRT deficiency impairs purine salvage increasing de novo purine synthesis and uric acid production",
        "Xanthine oxidase deficiency increases uric acid",
        "Adenine phosphoribosyltransferase (APRT) deficiency leads to decreased uric acid",
        "PRPP synthetase hypoactivity causes gout"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "HGPRT deficiency prevents salvage of hypoxanthine/guanine, causing increased PRPP and de novo purine synthesis, leading to excess uric acid and neurobehavioral abnormalities characteristic of Lesch–Nyhan.",
      "trap": "Xanthine oxidase inhibition decreases uric acid (allopurinol action).",
      "related": "Allopurinol reduces uric acid production but neurologic symptoms persist in Lesch–Nyhan.",
      "tags": ["purine-metabolism","lesch-nyhan","hgprt"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0009",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Oxidative Phosphorylation",
      "topic": "Mitochondrial Disorders",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient has lactic acidosis and muscle weakness due to mitochondrial dysfunction. Which feature distinguishes mitochondrial DNA–encoded disorders from nuclear gene defects in inheritance patterns?",
      "choices": [
        "Maternal inheritance and heteroplasmy leading to variable expression among offspring",
        "Strict Mendelian dominant transmission only",
        "Y-chromosome inheritance pattern",
        "Equal nuclear and mtDNA inheritance preventing variation"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Mitochondrial DNA is maternally inherited; heteroplasmy (mixture of normal and mutant mtDNA) causes variable penetrance and severity among siblings.",
      "trap": "Not Mendelian; paternal mtDNA contribution is negligible.",
      "related": "Examples include MELAS and Leber hereditary optic neuropathy.",
      "tags": ["mitochondria","heteroplasmy","lactic-acidosis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0010",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Hormone Action",
      "topic": "Steroid Hormones",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "Glucocorticoids act via intracellular receptors to alter gene transcription. Which mechanism underlies rapid metabolic effects of cortisol in liver during fasting?",
      "choices": [
        "Upregulation of gluconeogenic enzymes (PEPCK) increasing gluconeogenesis",
        "Immediate activation of insulin receptor tyrosine kinase",
        "Rapid stimulation of glycolysis via hexokinase",
        "Direct inhibition of glycogen phosphorylase"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Cortisol upregulates gluconeogenic enzymes (e.g., PEPCK, G6Pase) increasing hepatic glucose production during fasting; these are genomic effects requiring transcriptional changes.",
      "trap": "Cortisol is generally antagonistic to insulin's anabolic actions.",
      "related": "Chronic excess cortisol causes hyperglycemia and insulin resistance.",
      "tags": ["glucocorticoid","gluconeogenesis","hormone-action"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0011",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Carbohydrate Metabolism",
      "topic": "Glycogen Storage Diseases",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "An infant has severe fasting hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis with hepatomegaly. Deficiency of which enzyme in glycogen metabolism is most consistent with these findings (von Gierke disease)?",
      "choices": [
        "Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency",
        "Glycogen phosphorylase deficiency (McArdle) primarily affects muscle",
        "Debranching enzyme deficiency causes mild hypoglycemia only",
        "Branching enzyme deficiency causes cardiomyopathy only"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "von Gierke (GSD I) is deficiency of glucose-6-phosphatase causing impaired hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis, resulting in severe fasting hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, and hepatomegaly.",
      "trap": "McArdle affects muscle causing exercise intolerance; debranching (Cori) and branching (Andersen) have different presentations.",
      "related": "Management includes frequent feedings and cornstarch therapy.",
      "tags": ["gsd","glucose-6-phosphatase","hypoglycemia"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "BIOC0012",
      "subject": "Biochemistry",
      "chapter": "Membrane Biochemistry",
      "topic": "Lipoproteins",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient with familial hypercholesterolemia has elevated LDL and early atherosclerosis. Which receptor-mediated process is defective and how does this cause high LDL?",
      "choices": [
        "LDL receptor–mediated endocytosis in hepatocytes is defective leading to reduced clearance of LDL from plasma",
        "HDL-mediated reverse cholesterol transport is defective causing high LDL directly",
        "Chylomicron secretion is increased causing LDL accumulation",
        "Lipoprotein lipase overactivity elevates LDL"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Defective hepatic LDL receptor or its function reduces uptake of LDL particles, increasing circulating LDL and accelerating atherosclerosis; statins upregulate LDL receptors.",
      "trap": "HDL issues relate to reverse transport but familial hypercholesterolemia centers on LDL receptor mutations.",
      "related": "PCSK9 inhibitors enhance LDL receptor recycling to lower LDL.",
      "tags": ["ldl-receptor","familial-hypercholesterolemia","atherosclerosis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    }

  "microbiology.json": [
    {
      "id": "MICR0001",
      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Bacteriology",
      "topic": "Gram Stain",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A clinical isolate from a patient with community-acquired pneumonia grows on blood agar and shows alpha-hemolysis with optochin sensitivity. Which organism is most likely and what is a key virulence factor?",
      "choices": [
        "Streptococcus pneumoniae; polysaccharide capsule",
        "Streptococcus pyogenes; M protein",
        "Staphylococcus aureus; coagulase",
        "Haemophilus influenzae; fimbriae"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "S. pneumoniae is alpha-hemolytic and optochin-sensitive; its polysaccharide capsule is a major virulence factor enabling immune evasion.",
      "trap": "S. pyogenes is beta-hemolytic and bacitracin-sensitive, not optochin-sensitive.",
      "related": "Capsular polysaccharide is basis of pneumococcal vaccines.",
      "tags": ["s.pneumoniae","pneumonia","capsule"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "MICR0002",
      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Virology",
      "topic": "Viral Structure",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A virus is isolated that contains negative-sense segmented RNA and replicates in the nucleus, displaying antigenic shift potential. Which family does it most likely belong to and what clinical implication does segment reassortment have?",
      "choices": [
        "Orthomyxoviridae (influenza); segment reassortment enables antigenic shift causing pandemic strains",
        "Paramyxoviridae; antigenic shift is via point mutation only",
        "Picornaviridae; segmentation allows reassortment",
        "Adenoviridae; DNA viruses reassort frequently"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Orthomyxoviruses (influenza A) are negative-sense segmented RNA viruses capable of reassortment (antigenic shift) leading to novel surface antigens and potential pandemics.",
      "trap": "Paramyxoviruses are nonsegmented negative-sense RNA viruses (antigenic drift via mutation, not reassortment).",
      "related": "Influenza antigenic drift vs shift: drift causes seasonal variation, shift can cause pandemics.",
      "tags": ["influenza","reassortment","antigenic-shift"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {

      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Mycology",
      "topic": "Opportunistic Fungi",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,

      "choices": [
        "Mucor or Rhizopus species; diabetic ketoacidosis or neutropenia",
        "Aspergillus fumigatus; poorly controlled diabetes reduces risk",
        "Candida albicans; superficial mucosal infection only",
        "Cryptococcus neoformans; associated with hyperglycemia only"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Mucorales (Mucor/Rhizopus) show broad, nonseptate hyphae with wide-angle branching and invade in diabetic ketoacidosis and immunosuppression; they can cause rhinocerebral and pulmonary disease.",
      "trap": "Aspergillus shows narrow septate hyphae with acute-angle branching; Candida is yeast/pseudohyphae.",
      "related": "Mucormycosis requires urgent therapy including amphotericin B and surgical debridement.",
      "tags": ["mucormycosis","fungus","immunocompromised"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "MICR0004",
      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Bacterial Pathogenesis",
      "topic": "Toxins",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient develops severe watery diarrhea after antibiotic-induced C. difficile overgrowth. Which C. difficile virulence factor disrupts host cytoskeleton leading to mucosal damage and diarrhea?",
      "choices": [
        "Toxin B (glucosylates Rho regulatory proteins) leading to actin cytoskeleton disruption",
        "Toxin A functioning as an enterotoxin binding gangliosides",
        "Shiga toxin cleaves rRNA causing paralysis",
        "Cholera toxin ADP-ribosylates Gi proteins"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "C. difficile toxin B inactivates Rho GTPases by glucosylation disrupting actin cytoskeleton; toxin A also contributes but toxin B is highly cytotoxic.",
      "trap": "Cholera toxin causes secretory diarrhea via cAMP; Shiga toxin damages endothelium and causes bloody diarrhea/hemolytic-uremic syndrome.",
      "related": "C. difficile pseudomembranous colitis diagnosis includes toxin testing; treat with oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin.",
      "tags": ["c.diff","toxin-b","colitis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "MICR0005",
      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Parasitology Overlap",
      "topic": "Malaria Lifecycle",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A traveler returns with cyclic fevers and hemolytic anemia; peripheral smear shows ring forms in RBCs. Which species commonly causes tertian (48-hour) fevers and which diagnostic test confirms species identification?",
      "choices": [
        "Plasmodium falciparum or vivax; species identification via thick and thin blood smears with Giemsa stain—P. falciparum shows multiple rings and crescent gametocytes",
        "Trypanosoma brucei via blood smear only",
        "Leishmania via bone marrow aspiration exclusively",
        "Entamoeba histolytica cysts in stool diagnose malaria"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Malaria diagnosis is by thick (sensitivity) and thin (speciation) Giemsa-stained blood smears; P. falciparum shows multiple ring forms and crescent gametocytes; fever periodicity helps species differentiation (P. vivax/ovale tertian).",
      "trap": "Other protozoa produce different clinical pictures; stool ova/parasite tests are irrelevant for malaria.",
      "related": "Rapid antigen tests (RDTs) can supplement microscopy in resource-limited settings.",
      "tags": ["malaria","gemsia","parasite-diagnosis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "MICR0006",
      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Antimicrobial Resistance",
      "topic": "Beta-lactamases",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A urinary E. coli isolate is resistant to third-generation cephalosporins but susceptible to carbapenems. Which enzyme family mediates this pattern and what molecular mechanism restores susceptibility?",
      "choices": [
        "Extended-spectrum β-lactamases (ESBLs) hydrolyze cephalosporins; β-lactamase inhibitor combinations or carbapenems bypass ESBL hydrolysis",
        "AmpC β-lactamases are inhibited by clavulanate restoring susceptibility to cephalosporins",
        "Carbapenemases are susceptible to cephalosporins",
        "Efflux pumps only mediate this pattern"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "ESBLs hydrolyze penicillins and cephalosporins but are typically inhibited by carbapenems; β-lactamase inhibitors variably affect ESBLs—carbapenems are often effective.",
      "trap": "AmpC is not inhibited by clavulanate and carbapenems still often effective; carbapenemases would confer carbapenem resistance.",
      "related": "Detection of ESBLs guides therapy to carbapenems or specific inhibitor combinations.",
      "tags": ["esbl","beta-lactamase","antimicrobial-resistance"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "topic": "Culture Media",

        "Only aerobic culture bottles on a single sample suffice",
        "Urine culture alone identifies Candida in bloodstream reliably",
        "Swab the skin surface near the line instead of blood cultures"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Paired blood cultures increase sensitivity; yeast may require specialized media and can be slow-growing—thus prolonged incubation and fungal media increase detection of candidemia.",
      "trap": "Single cultures or non-blood sites are insufficient for diagnosing bloodstream infection.",
      "related": "Early diagnosis guides antifungal therapy; consider removal of infected catheters.",

      "chapter": "Immunology Overlap",
      "topic": "Vaccine Types",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A new vaccine uses a nonreplicating adenoviral vector expressing a viral antigen. What type of immune responses does this platform primarily elicit and what safety advantage does nonreplication confer?",
      "choices": [
        "Strong cellular (CD8) and humoral immunity due to intracellular antigen expression; nonreplicating vectors avoid uncontrolled dissemination and minimize risk in immunocompromised hosts",
        "Only mucosal IgA responses without T-cell activation",
        "Induces tolerance rather than immunity",
        "Causes active infection due to replication"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Adenoviral vectors deliver transgenes intracellularly enabling MHC I presentation and CD8 responses plus antibody generation; nonreplicating vectors cannot produce progeny virus, improving safety.",
      "trap": "Nonreplicating vectors do not cause active spreading infection—important in immunocompromised individuals.",
      "related": "Viral vectored vaccines used in several platforms (e.g., Ebola, SARS-CoV-2 designs).",
      "tags": ["vaccine","adenovector","cellular-immunity"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Sterilization",
      "topic": "Autoclaving",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A surgical instrument must be sterilized to eliminate spores. Which sterilization modality reliably kills bacterial spores and by which mechanism?",
      "choices": [
        "Moist heat autoclaving (steam under pressure) denatures proteins and nucleic acids, killing spores",
        "Dry heat at low temperature sterilizes by osmotic disruption",
        "Alcohol wipes eliminate spores effectively",
        "UV light penetrates instruments to kill spores comprehensively"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Autoclave uses pressurized steam (121°C, 15 psi) to penetrate and denature critical biomolecules, effectively killing spores; alcohol and UV are insufficient for sterilization of instruments.",
      "trap": "Alcohol is a disinfectant but not sporicidal; dry heat requires higher temperatures and longer times.",
      "related": "Sterilization choice depends on instrument heat tolerance and required sterility assurance.",
      "tags": ["sterilization","autoclave","infection-control"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
