      "id": "MICR0010",
      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Zoonoses",
      "topic": "Rickettsial Disease",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A traveler presents with fever, headache, and an eschar with regional lymphadenopathy after a tick bite. Serology suggests Rickettsia species. Which antibiotic is first-line therapy and what is the intracellular localization that dictates this choice?",
      "choices": [
        "Doxycycline; Rickettsia are obligate intracellular organisms inhibiting mitochondrial protein synthesis",
        "Penicillin; Rickettsia are extracellular Gram-positive cocci",
        "Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole; Rickettsia lack folate pathways",
        "Amphotericin B; Rickettsia are fungal"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Doxycycline penetrates host cells and targets bacterial ribosomes, effective against obligate intracellular organisms like Rickettsia; beta-lactams are ineffective as intracellular localization and lack of typical cell wall exposure limit activity.",
      "trap": "Avoid assuming extracellular pathogens—intracellular location requires agents with intracellular penetration.",
      "related": "Doxycycline is used for RMSF, scrub typhus, and other rickettsial infections.",
      "tags": ["rickettsia","doxycycline","zoonosis"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Diagnostics",
      "topic": "PCR",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A hospital lab uses PCR to detect Clostridioides difficile toxin genes from stool samples. What advantage does PCR offer over toxin immunoassays, and what is a potential limitation clinically?",
      "choices": [
        "Higher sensitivity detecting gene presence; may detect colonization (genes present) without active toxin expression causing overdiagnosis",
        "Lower sensitivity but perfect specificity compared to immunoassays",
        "PCR quantifies active toxin levels directly correlating with disease severity",
        "PCR replaces the need for clinical correlation entirely"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "PCR is highly sensitive for detecting toxin genes but cannot distinguish between colonization and active toxin production; clinical correlation is necessary to avoid over-treatment.",
      "trap": "PCR does not measure active toxin; false positives from colonization are possible.",
      "related": "Testing algorithms often include GDH and toxin assays in combination with PCR.",
      "tags": ["pcr","diagnostics","c.diff"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "id": "MICR0012",
      "subject": "Microbiology",
      "chapter": "Public Health Microbiology",
      "topic": "Antibiotic Stewardship",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient with uncomplicated acute bronchitis is viral in origin. What stewardship principle should clinicians apply regarding antibiotics and what is the rationale?",
      "choices": [
        "Avoid antibiotics to reduce resistance selection and unnecessary side effects because most acute bronchitis is viral and self-limited",
        "Prescribe broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent secondary bacterial infection in all cases",
        "Use vancomycin empirically for all respiratory infections",
        "Initiate prophylactic antifungals to prevent superinfection"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Antibiotics are not indicated for viral bronchitis; unnecessary use selects for resistant organisms and exposes patients to adverse events, undermining stewardship goals.",
      "trap": "Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics increase resistance and are not justified without evidence of bacterial infection.",
      "related": "Stewardship programs promote diagnostic testing and targeted therapy.",
      "tags": ["stewardship","respiratory-infection","public-health"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    }

    {
      "id": "IMM0001",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Innate Immunity",
      "topic": "Toll-Like Receptors",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient with recurrent Gram-negative sepsis has diminished cytokine responses. A defect in which family of receptors that recognize LPS likely impairs TNF and IL-1 production by macrophages?",
      "choices": [
        "Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) pathway recognizes LPS via MD-2/CD14 co-receptor",
        "T-cell receptor directly recognizes LPS",
        "B-cell receptor binds LPS causing cytokine release",
        "Complement receptor CR1 mediates LPS signaling"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "TLR4 (with MD-2 and CD14) recognizes LPS triggering MyD88/TRIF-dependent pathways producing proinflammatory cytokines; defects impair innate responses to Gram-negative bacteria.",
      "trap": "TCR/BCR are adaptive antigen receptors, not pattern recognition receptors for LPS.",
      "related": "TLR polymorphisms influence sepsis susceptibility and responses to adjuvants.",
      "tags": ["tlr4","innate-immunity","lps"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0002",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Adaptive Immunity",
      "topic": "B Cell Maturation",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A 10-year-old has recurrent bacterial infections and absent germinal centers on lymph node biopsy. Which immunodeficiency fits this finding and what adaptive immune function is impaired?",
      "choices": [
        "X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton's) with absent B cells and impaired antibody production",
        "SCID due to T-cell defect only with intact B-cell function",
        "Common variable immunodeficiency with normal B-cell numbers only",
        "Hyper-IgE syndrome with increased antibody production"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Bruton's agammaglobulinemia (BTK deficiency) causes lack of mature B cells and absent germinal centers leading to profound antibody deficiency and recurrent pyogenic infections.",
      "trap": "SCID affects both T and B cells; CVID may have variable germinal center changes; hyper-IgE has high IgE not absent germinal centers.",
      "related": "Treatment includes immunoglobulin replacement and infection prophylaxis.",
      "tags": ["btk","agammaglobulinemia","humoral-immunity"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0003",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Hypersensitivity",
      "topic": "Type IV Hypersensitivity",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A contact dermatitis develops after topical exposure to nickel. Which immune mechanism and cell type mediate this delayed hypersensitivity reaction?",
      "choices": [
        "Th1 CD4+ T cells and macrophage activation releasing IFN-γ (cell-mediated, type IV)",
        "IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation (type I)",
        "Immune complex deposition in vessels (type III)",
        "Complement activation via classical pathway (type II)"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Contact hypersensitivity is T-cell mediated (type IV) where haptens like nickel form complexes that activate Th1 cells, recruiting macrophages and causing delayed skin inflammation.",
      "trap": "Type I is immediate IgE mediated; contact dermatitis is delayed cell-mediated immunity.",
      "related": "Patch testing identifies specific contact allergens in dermatology.",
      "tags": ["type-iv","contact-dermatitis","t-cell"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0004",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Complement System",
      "topic": "Complement Deficiencies",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient has recurrent Neisseria infections. Deficiency of which complement components most commonly predisposes to invasive Neisseria infections and why?",
      "choices": [
        "Terminal complement components (C5–C9) impair MAC formation needed to lyse Neisseria",
        "C3 deficiency specifically increases Neisseria infections only",
        "C1q deficiency causes susceptibility to Neisseria exclusively",
        "Factor H deficiency prevents Neisseria binding"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "MAC (membrane attack complex) components C5–C9 are essential for forming pores to lyse Neisseria; deficiencies predispose to recurrent invasive infections by Neisseria species.",
      "trap": "C3 deficiency causes broad pyogenic infections and immune complex disease; terminal complement defects are classically linked to Neisseria.",
      "related": "Vaccination against Neisseria meningitidis is important in complement-deficient patients.",
      "tags": ["complement","mac","neisseria"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0005",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Autoimmunity",
      "topic": "Autoantibody Testing",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient with systemic lupus erythematosus has positive anti-dsDNA and hypocomplementemia during a flare. How do immune complexes contribute to organ damage in SLE?",
      "choices": [
        "Immune complexes deposit in tissues activating complement and recruiting inflammatory cells causing tissue injury",
        "Autoantibodies directly lyse cells via perforin release from T cells",
        "Immune complexes neutralize complement preventing inflammation",
        "Presence of immune complexes indicates resolution of disease"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "In SLE, circulating immune complexes deposit in kidneys, skin, and vessels, activating complement and recruiting neutrophils/macrophages leading to inflammatory tissue damage.",
      "trap": "Immune complexes promote, not prevent, inflammation in immune complex–mediated disease.",
      "related": "Complement levels fall during active immune complex–mediated flares.",
      "tags": ["immune-complex","sle","complement"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0006",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Cell-Mediated Immunity",
      "topic": "TH17 Cells",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient with chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis has impaired IL-17 responses. Which immune function is compromised and what infections are associated with Th17 deficiency?",
      "choices": [
        "Recruitment of neutrophils to mucosal surfaces is impaired leading to susceptibility to Candida and mucosal bacterial infections",
        "Impaired antibody class switching causing viral susceptibility",
        "Excessive IL-10 secretion preventing any infection",
        "Enhanced NK cell killing preventing fungal infections"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Th17 cells produce IL-17 and IL-22 promoting neutrophil recruitment and mucosal defense; defects predispose to mucocutaneous candidiasis and certain extracellular bacterial infections.",
      "trap": "Th17 is distinct from Th1/Th2 roles; antibody class switching is more linked to Tfh and CD4 help.",
      "related": "STAT3 mutations (Job syndrome) impair Th17 and lead to mucocutaneous infections and eczema.",
      "tags": ["th17","il-17","fungal-immunity"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0007",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Vaccinology",
      "topic": "Adjuvants",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "An adjuvant included in a subunit vaccine enhances immune responses by activating innate pathways. What is the primary role of an adjuvant in vaccination?",
      "choices": [
        "Enhance innate immune activation and antigen presentation, boosting adaptive immune responses",
        "Replace antigenic protein to induce tolerance",
        "Inhibit dendritic cell maturation to reduce inflammation",
        "Provide long-term antigen persistence without immune activation"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Adjuvants stimulate innate immune receptors and promote antigen uptake and presentation by APCs, thereby enhancing T and B cell responses to vaccine antigens.",
      "trap": "Adjuvants do not aim to induce tolerance or suppress DCs; they augment immunity.",
      "related": "Alum and newer TLR agonists serve as adjuvants in various vaccines.",
      "tags": ["adjuvant","vaccine","immunology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0008",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Transplantation Immunology",
      "topic": "Graft Rejection",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A renal transplant recipient develops acute rejection within weeks with perivascular infiltrates and endothelial damage. Which immune cells and pathways mediate acute cellular graft rejection?",
      "choices": [
        "Host T lymphocytes recognizing donor alloantigens via direct and indirect pathways leading to cytotoxicity and cytokine-mediated injury",
        "Preformed antibodies only cause hyperacute rejection within minutes",
        "NK cells alone mediate chronic rejection months later",
        "Complement activation without cellular immunity causes acute cellular rejection"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Acute cellular rejection is T cell–mediated: host T cells recognize donor MHC and mount a cell-mediated cytotoxic response with cytokines causing endothelial and parenchymal injury; humoral mechanisms can also be involved in acute rejection.",
      "trap": "Hyperacute rejection is antibody-mediated immediate; chronic rejection involves vascular fibrosis over longer term.",
      "related": "Immunosuppression targets T-cell activation (calcineurin inhibitors, anti-proliferative agents).",
      "tags": ["transplant","acute-rejection","tcell"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "IMM0009",
      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Tolerance",
      "topic": "Central vs Peripheral Tolerance",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Autoimmunity can result from failed tolerance mechanisms. Which central tolerance mechanism in the thymus eliminates self-reactive T cells?",
      "choices": [
        "Negative selection of thymocytes that strongly recognize self-peptides presented by medullary thymic epithelial cells",
        "Positive selection eliminating non-self-reactive T cells",
        "Peripheral anergy in lymph nodes is central mechanism",
        "Clonal expansion in bone marrow"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Negative selection deletes T cells with high affinity for self-peptide–MHC complexes in the thymus, preventing autoimmunity; positive selection ensures MHC restriction.",
      "trap": "Peripheral tolerance mechanisms (anergy, Tregs) act outside thymus but are not central selection.",
      "related": "AIRE mutations impair expression of tissue-restricted antigens leading to autoimmune polyendocrine syndromes.",
      "tags": ["tolerance","negative-selection","thymus"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "topic": "Flow Cytometry",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,

      "choices": [

        "Elevated NK cells with reduced B cells suggests X-linked SCID",
        "Flow cytometry cannot assess lymphocyte subsets"
      ],
      "answer": 0,

      "trap": "Presence of B cells does not equate to functional humoral immunity in SCID.",

    },
    {

      "subject": "Immunology",
      "chapter": "Immune Checkpoints",
      "topic": "Cancer Immunotherapy",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "Checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1/PD-L1 revolutionize cancer therapy. What is the mechanism of action and what immune-related adverse event might occur?",
      "choices": [
        "Block negative regulatory signals restoring T-cell antitumor activity; can provoke autoimmune-like colitis or endocrinopathies",
        "Stimulate PD-1 to reduce autoimmunity during cancer therapy",
        "Directly lyse tumor cells without immune involvement",
        "Only enhance B-cell antibody responses"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors remove inhibitory signals on T cells enhancing antitumor immunity but may break peripheral tolerance causing immune-related adverse events like colitis, pneumonitis, thyroiditis, or hypophysitis.",
      "trap": "They do not directly lyse tumor cells chemically; immune-mediated effects cause both efficacy and toxicity.",
      "related": "Management of severe immune-related events includes immunosuppression (corticosteroids).",
      "tags": ["pd-1","immunotherapy","adverse-events"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      ],

      "reference": null

      "choices": [
        "Direct mucosal invasion with cytolysis and flask-shaped ulcers; metronidazole for tissue disease plus luminal agent (paromomycin)",
        "Enterotoxin-mediated secretory diarrhea treated with cholera vaccine",
        "Surface attachment without invasion treated by albendazole",
        "Transmission via inhalation treated by amphotericin B"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "E. histolytica invades intestinal mucosa causing dysentery and can disseminate to liver causing abscesses; metronidazole treats tissue disease while luminal agents eradicate intestinal carriage.",
      "trap": "Luminal agents alone do not treat invasive liver abscess; parasite ingestion is fecal–oral, not inhalation.",
      "related": "Serology and imaging help detect hepatic involvement.",

    },

        "Adhesion by plasmodium in RBCs treated with artemisinin",
        "Giardia produces secreted toxin responsive to antitoxin therapy"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Giardia adheres to intestinal epithelium causing villous blunting and disaccharidase deficiency leading to steatorrhea; nitroimidazoles (metronidazole) are effective.",
      "trap": "Giardia does not invade mucosa or produce classic cytotoxins causing blood; antibiotics for bacterial enteritis are not appropriate.",

      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,

        "Snail control, sanitation improvements to prevent contamination, and mass drug administration with praziquantel",
        "Rely solely on vaccination of all exposed individuals",
        "Apply insecticide-treated bed nets only",
        "Boil drinking water exclusively to control schistosomiasis"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Schistosomiasis control relies on reducing snail intermediate hosts, improving sanitation to prevent human contamination of water, and mass treatment with praziquantel; no widely used vaccine exists.",
      "trap": "Bed nets prevent mosquito-borne diseases, not snail-transmitted trematodes.",
      "related": "Integrated measures are key to sustainable control in endemic regions.",

      "reference": null
    },

        "Hymenolepis produces eosinophilic pneumonia only"

  "pathology.json": [
    {
      "id": "PATH0001",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Inflammation",
      "topic": "Acute vs Chronic",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A tissue sample shows abundant neutrophils with edema and vascular dilation. Does this pattern reflect acute or chronic inflammation and which mediator is primarily responsible for vasodilation?",
      "choices": [
        "Acute inflammation; histamine and nitric oxide mediate vasodilation",
        "Chronic inflammation; interferon-γ is vasodilatory",
        "Granulomatous inflammation only with TNF-α causing vasodilation",
        "Resolution phase characterized by neutrophils only"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Acute inflammation features neutrophils, edema, and vasodilation mediated by histamine, prostaglandins, and NO; chronic inflammation shows lymphocytes, macrophages, and often fibrosis.",
      "trap": "Interferon-γ is key for macrophage activation in chronic inflammation, not primary vasodilator.",
      "related": "Timing and cellular composition help differentiate inflammatory stages histologically.",
      "tags": ["inflammation","acute","histology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PATH0002",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Neoplasia",
      "topic": "Tumor Grading",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A tumor biopsy shows pleomorphic cells with high mitotic index and loss of glandular differentiation. Which features indicate high-grade malignancy and how do grade and stage differ in prognostic importance?",
      "choices": [
        "High mitotic activity, pleomorphism, and poor differentiation indicate high grade; stage (extent of spread) generally correlates more with prognosis than grade",
        "High differentiation and low mitotic count indicate high grade",
        "Grade equals stage and both are identical prognostically",
        "Only histologic subtype determines prognosis regardless of stage"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "High-grade tumors show pleomorphism, high mitoses, and poor differentiation; staging (tumor size, nodal involvement, metastasis) typically provides stronger prognostic information though grade also matters.",
      "trap": "Grade and stage are distinct: grade assesses cellular atypia; stage assesses extent.",
      "related": "Both are used to guide therapy decisions.",
      "tags": ["tumor-grade","prognosis","oncology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PATH0003",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Cell Injury",
      "topic": "Reversible Injury",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Cells exposed to transient ischemia show cellular swelling and blebbing. What ultrastructural change occurs early in reversible injury and which organelle is most susceptible to irreversible damage if hypoxia persists?",
      "choices": [
        "Cell swelling and membrane blebs occur early; mitochondria sustain irreversible damage leading to loss of ATP production",
        "Nuclear fragmentation occurs first and is reversible",
        "Lysosomal proliferation always precedes any injury reversal",
        "Ribosomes are spared and indicate irreversible damage when intact"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Early reversible injury shows cellular swelling due to Na/K pump failure; prolonged hypoxia causes mitochondrial permeability transition and irreversible damage culminating in necrosis.",
      "trap": "Nuclear fragmentation (karyorrhexis) indicates irreversible injury (necrosis/apoptosis), not early reversible changes.",
      "related": "Reperfusion can salvage tissue before irreversible mitochondrial damage occurs.",
      "tags": ["cell-injury","ischemia","mitochondria"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PATH0004",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Hemodynamics",
      "topic": "Shock",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "In septic shock, microvascular alterations cause tissue hypoperfusion despite elevated cardiac output in early stages. Which microcirculatory change contributes to organ dysfunction in sepsis?",
      "choices": [
        "Endothelial activation with increased permeability and microthrombi causing heterogeneous perfusion and tissue hypoxia",
        "Uniform vasoconstriction restoring perfusion to vital organs only",
        "Decreased NO production leading to increased vascular tone only",
        "Enhanced lymphatic drainage preventing edema"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Sepsis causes endothelial activation, glycocalyx disruption, and disseminated microthrombosis with shunting and heterogeneous flow leading to tissue hypoxia despite overall high output; NO is often increased causing vasodilation.",
      "trap": "Assuming uniform vasoconstriction is incorrect; sepsis features vasodilation and microcirculatory dysfunction.",
      "related": "Therapy targets source control, fluids, and vasopressors to support perfusion.",
      "tags": ["sepsis","microthrombi","endothelium"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PATH0005",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Renal Pathology",
      "topic": "Acute Tubular Necrosis",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient has oliguria after hypotensive shock and nephrotoxins; urine microscopy shows muddy brown granular casts. Which structure is primarily injured in ischemic ATN and what is the expected fractional excretion of sodium (FENa)?",
      "choices": [
        "Proximal tubule and thick ascending limb are injured; FENa typically >2% reflecting intrinsic tubular damage",
        "Glomerulus is selectively injured with low FENa <1%",
        "Collecting duct necrosis causes FENa <0.1%",
        "Interstitial nephritis shows muddy brown casts and high FENa always"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Ischemic/toxic ATN injures highly metabolic proximal tubules and TAL; tubules cannot reabsorb sodium leading to elevated FENa (>2%) and muddy brown casts on urine sediment.",
      "trap": "Prerenal azotemia shows low FENa (<1%) due to intact tubules conserving sodium.",
      "related": "Management is supportive with attention to fluid balance and avoiding nephrotoxins.",
      "tags": ["atn","fenA","nephrology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PATH0006",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Infectious Disease",
      "topic": "Granulomatous Inflammation",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A lung biopsy shows caseating granulomas with central necrosis. Which pathogens are classically associated with caseous granulomas and which immune cell orchestrates granuloma formation?",
      "choices": [
        "Mycobacterium tuberculosis; Th1 CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ activate macrophages",
        "Streptococcus pyogenes; B cells form granulomas",
        "Candida albicans; neutrophils form caseating granulomas",
        "Aspergillus produces caseating granulomas driven by eosinophils"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "TB induces caseating granulomas driven by Th1 CD4+ cells producing IFN-γ that activates macrophages to form epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells with central necrosis.",
      "trap": "Granulomas from other agents may be noncaseating; neutrophils/eosinophils are not central in classic TB granulomas.",
      "related": "Sarcoidosis features noncaseating granulomas often with different clinical context.",
      "tags": ["granuloma","tb","th1"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PATH0007",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Hematopathology",
      "topic": "Reactive vs Clonal Lymphocytosis",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient has persistent lymphocytosis with smudge cells on peripheral smear and splenomegaly. Flow cytometry shows CD5+ CD19+ CD23+ B cells. Which diagnosis fits and what is the typical prognosis?",
      "choices": [
        "Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL); often indolent but may progress and require therapy",
        "Acute lymphoblastic leukemia with aggressive course in adults",
        "Reactive viral lymphocytosis with spontaneous resolution",
        "Hairy cell leukemia with CD23 negativity"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "CLL is characterized by mature B cells coexpressing CD5+ and CD23+ with smudge cells and splenomegaly; clinical course is variable often indolent but can transform to aggressive lymphoma.",
      "trap": "ALL shows blasts and different immunophenotype; hairy cell leukemia is CD11c+/CD25+/TRAP+ and often CD23−.",
      "related": "Management ranges from watchful waiting to chemoimmunotherapy depending on stage.",
      "tags": ["cll","flow-cytometry","hematopathology"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PATH0008",
      "subject": "Pathology",
      "chapter": "Toxicology",
      "topic": "Acetaminophen Toxicity",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient with acetaminophen overdose develops centrilobular hepatic necrosis. Which metabolic pathway produces the toxic metabolite and what antidote replenishes glutathione?",
      "choices": [
        "Cytochrome P450 (CYP2E1) produces NAPQI; N-acetylcysteine replenishes glutathione",
        "Direct phase II glucuronidation produces toxin reversed by vitamin K",
        "Aspirin coadministration prevents toxicity",
        "Activated charcoal reverses toxic metabolite after 72 hours"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "NAPQI is formed via CYP-mediated oxidation; glutathione conjugates and detoxifies it—N-acetylcysteine restores glutathione stores and prevents hepatic injury if given timely.",
      "trap": "Phase II pathways normally detoxify acetaminophen but are overwhelmed in overdose; charcoal is effective early but not late.",
      "related": "Timing of NAC administration is critical; Rumack–Matthew nomogram guides therapy.",
      "tags": ["acetaminophen","napqi","nac"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "subject": "Pathology",

      "topic": "Paraneoplastic Syndromes",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient with squamous cell carcinoma of the lung presents with hypercalcemia. Which paraneoplastic mediator is most likely responsible and what mechanism elevates calcium?",
      "choices": [
        "PTH-related peptide (PTHrP) produced by tumor causing bone resorption and renal calcium retention",
        "ADH causing hypercalcemia by water retention",
        "ACTH causing hypercalcemia via cortisol",
        "Insulin-like growth factor lowering calcium"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Squamous carcinomas often secrete PTHrP mimicking PTH actions stimulating osteoclast-mediated bone resorption and increasing renal calcium reabsorption causing humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy.",
      "trap": "SIADH (ADH excess) causes hyponatremia, not hypercalcemia.",
      "related": "Management includes bisphosphonates and treating underlying malignancy.",
      "tags": ["paraneoplastic","pthrp","hypercalcemia"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

        "Calcified stable plaque always ruptures less frequently",
        "Thick fibrous cap with abundant smooth muscle prevents rupture",
        "Plaque size alone dictates rupture regardless of cap integrity"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Thin-cap fibroatheromas with inflammatory cell infiltration and matrix degradation are prone to rupture exposing necrotic lipid core and triggering thrombosis; cap integrity is critical.",
      "trap": "Calcified plaques are often more stable; plaque composition matters more than size alone.",
      "related": "Inflammation-targeted therapies are explored to stabilize vulnerable plaques.",
      "tags": ["atherosclerosis","plaque-rupture","mi"],

      "topic": "Cirrhosis",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "Cirrhosis results in portal hypertension. Which histologic changes characterize cirrhosis and how do they lead to increased portal venous pressure?",
      "choices": [
        "Diffuse hepatic fibrosis with regenerative nodules distort hepatic architecture increasing intrahepatic vascular resistance",
        "Fatty change alone causes portal hypertension without fibrosis",
        "Sinusoidal dilation reduces resistance lowering portal pressure",
        "Periportal inflammation exclusively reduces portal flow"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Cirrhosis involves bridging fibrosis and regenerative nodules that distort sinusoids and portal triads increasing resistance to portal flow which elevates portal venous pressure.",
      "trap": "Steatosis alone does not cause portal hypertension unless progressed to fibrosis/cirrhosis.",
      "related": "Complications include varices, splenomegaly, and ascites.",

        "Ewing sarcoma expressing myogenin exclusively"

  "pharmacology.json": [
    {
      "id": "PHARM0001",
      "subject": "Pharmacology",
      "chapter": "Pharmacokinetics",
      "topic": "Half-life and Steady State",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A drug has a half-life of 8 hours and is administered at regular intervals. Approximately after how many half-lives (and hours) will steady state be achieved and what clinical implication does this have for loading doses?",
      "choices": [
        "About 4–5 half-lives (~32–40 hours); use of a loading dose can achieve therapeutic levels rapidly",
        "One half-life (~8 hours) suffices for steady state always",
        "Steady state never achieved with continuous dosing",
        "Half-life irrelevant; steady state immediate after first dose"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Steady state is reached after ~4–5 half-lives; loading doses can promptly raise plasma concentration to therapeutic levels to avoid waiting multiple half-lives.",
      "trap": "Assuming immediate steady state neglects accumulation kinetics.",
      "related": "Renal impairment prolongs half-life requiring dose adjustments.",
      "tags": ["half-life","steady-state","dosing"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PHARM0002",
      "subject": "Pharmacology",
      "chapter": "Pharmacodynamics",
      "topic": "Receptor Agonists",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A new bronchodilator is a partial agonist at β2-adrenergic receptors. Compared to a full agonist, how does a partial agonist behave at high receptor occupancy and what are potential therapeutic advantages?",
      "choices": [
        "Produces submaximal response even at full receptor occupancy and can act as functional antagonist in presence of full agonist; advantage is lower overstimulation and side effects",
        "Produces greater maximal effect than full agonist",
        "Acts only as antagonist and has no intrinsic activity",
        "Has identical efficacy and safety as full agonist"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Partial agonists have intrinsic activity lower than full agonists, giving moderate responses and can attenuate effects of full agonists; therapeutically useful to reduce adverse effects linked to full receptor activation.",
      "trap": "Partial agonists are not inert antagonists; they have some agonist activity.",
      "related": "Buprenorphine is a partial μ-opioid agonist used in opioid dependence for this property.",
      "tags": ["partial-agonist","pharmacodynamics","beta2"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {

      "subject": "Pharmacology",
      "chapter": "Antibiotics",
      "topic": "Aminoglycosides",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A hospitalized patient on gentamicin develops rising creatinine and vestibular symptoms. Which toxicities are associated with aminoglycosides and how does dosing strategy mitigate nephrotoxicity?",
      "choices": [
        "Nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity (vestibular/cochlear); once-daily dosing exploits concentration-dependent killing with post-antibiotic effect to reduce toxicity",
        "Hepatotoxicity only with gentamicin",
        "Aminoglycosides cause bone marrow suppression primarily",
        "Renal toxicity is unrelated to dosing duration"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Aminoglycosides cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity; extended-interval dosing (once-daily) takes advantage of concentration-dependent bactericidal effects and post-antibiotic effect allowing renal recovery and reducing toxicity risk.",
      "trap": "Assuming hepatic or marrow toxicity is primary is incorrect for aminoglycosides.",
      "related": "Monitor drug levels and renal function routinely during therapy.",
      "tags": ["gentamicin","ototoxicity","nephrotoxicity"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PHARM0004",
      "subject": "Pharmacology",
      "chapter": "Cardiovascular Drugs",
      "topic": "ACE Inhibitors",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A patient with hypertension and diabetic nephropathy is started on an ACE inhibitor. What renal hemodynamic effect can cause an acute rise in serum creatinine, and which patients are at higher risk?",
      "choices": [
        "Efferent arteriolar dilation reduces GFR causing higher serum creatinine; patients with bilateral renal artery stenosis are at higher risk",
        "Afferent arteriolar constriction increases GFR producing lower creatinine",
        "ACE inhibitors directly damage tubular cells causing creatinine rise in all patients equally",
        "ACE inhibitors do not affect renal function"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "ACE inhibitors reduce angiotensin II–mediated efferent constriction lowering intraglomerular pressure and GFR causing creatinine rise; patients dependent on angiotensin II (bilateral RAS) are at high risk for marked decline.",
      "trap": "Not all patients have harmful creatinine rises; small increases can be acceptable and expected.",
      "related": "Monitor renal function and potassium after initiating ACE inhibitors.",
      "tags": ["ace-inhibitor","gfr","renal"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "difficulty": "Medium",

        "Trimethoprim increases vitamin K synthesis raising INR",
        "Sulfamethoxazole competes with warfarin at vitamin K epoxide reductase",
        "No interaction exists between these drugs"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "TMP-SMX inhibits CYP enzymes reducing warfarin clearance and may alter gut flora decreasing vitamin K synthesis, both enhancing warfarin effect and raising INR.",
      "trap": "Assuming TMP-SMX increases vitamin K is opposite; monitoring and dose adjustment of warfarin are necessary.",

    },

      "subject": "Pharmacology",
      "chapter": "Antihypertensives",
      "topic": "Beta-blockers",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A patient with asthma is prescribed a nonselective beta-blocker and develops bronchospasm. Which receptor selectivity is preferable in patients with reactive airway disease and why?",
      "choices": [
        "Cardioselective β1-blockers (e.g., metoprolol) are preferred because they spare β2-mediated bronchodilation at usual doses",
        "Nonselective β-blockers like propranolol are safest for asthma",
        "β2-selective blockers cause more bronchospasm",
        "Alpha-blockers are preferred to block bronchial receptors"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "β2 receptors mediate bronchodilation; nonselective β-blockers block β2 causing bronchospasm. Cardioselective β1-blockers reduce cardiac effects with less β2 blockade at therapeutic doses, preferable for asthmatics needing β-blockade.",
      "trap": "Even cardioselective agents can affect β2 at high doses; caution is advised.",
      "related": "Asthma patients often avoid nonselective β-blockers unless necessary with caution.",
      "tags": ["beta-blocker","asthma","selectivity"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "subject": "Pharmacology",
      "chapter": "Analgesics",
      "topic": "Opioids",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "An opioid-naïve patient receives morphine and becomes hypoventilatory with pinpoint pupils. What receptor mediates opioid analgesia and respiratory depression, and what is the reversal agent for life-threatening overdose?",
      "choices": [
        "Mu-opioid receptor mediates effects; naloxone is the antagonist that reverses overdose",
        "Kappa receptor mediates analgesia and naloxone is ineffective",
        "Delta receptor causes respiratory depression specifically",
        "NMDA receptor antagonists reverse opioid effects"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Mu receptors mediate analgesia, euphoria, and respiratory depression; naloxone is a competitive opioid antagonist that rapidly reverses respiratory depression in overdose.",
      "trap": "Kappa/delta have distinct effects but mu is primary for clinical analgesia and respiratory depression.",

      "reference": null

      "topic": "P2Y12 Inhibitors",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Clopidogrel requires hepatic activation to inhibit platelet P2Y12 receptors. A patient with loss-of-function CYP2C19 alleles experiences thrombotic events after PCI despite clopidogrel therapy. What is the pharmacogenetic implication and alternative therapy?",
      "choices": [
        "Poor metabolizers have reduced activation and efficacy; use prasugrel or ticagrelor which are less dependent on CYP2C19",
        "Increase clopidogrel dose in poor metabolizers to overcome deficiency reliably",
        "Switch to aspirin monotherapy regardless of stent type",
        "Genotype has no clinical relevance for antiplatelet therapy"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Clopidogrel is a prodrug activated by CYP2C19; poor metabolizers have reduced active metabolite and decreased antiplatelet effect. Prasugrel and ticagrelor provide more consistent platelet inhibition independent of CYP2C19 genotypes.",
      "trap": "Dose escalation may not reliably compensate; genotyping guides alternative therapy choice.",
      "related": "Pharmacogenetic testing can inform personalized antiplatelet strategies in high-risk patients.",

      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

        "Serotonin excess causes dystonia treated with SSRIs",
        "GABA deficiency causes dystonia managed with naloxone",
        "Dystonia reflects primary mood disorder requiring lithium"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Antipsychotics block D2 receptors in nigrostriatal pathway leading to EPS including dystonia; anticholinergics (benztropine) or antihistamines (diphenhydramine) can promptly reverse acute dystonic reactions.",
      "trap": "SSRIs and lithium do not address antipsychotic-induced dystonia acutely.",
      "related": "Tardive dyskinesia is a later, often irreversible effect requiring different management.",

      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "chapter": "Antimicrobials",
      "topic": "Fluoroquinolones",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Fluoroquinolones inhibit bacterial topoisomerases. Which enzymes are targeted and what adverse effect limits their use in certain populations?",
      "choices": [
        "DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV; tendon rupture and cartilage damage risks limit use in children and pregnant women",
        "RNA polymerase exclusively causing ototoxicity",
        "Peptidoglycan synthesis enzymes causing teratogenicity",
        "Folate synthesis enzymes causing anemia"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV impairing DNA replication; they are associated with tendonitis/rupture and cartilage toxicity concerns restricting pediatric and pregnant use when alternatives exist.",

  "publichealth.json": [
    {
      "id": "PH2023001",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Epidemiology",
      "topic": "Measures of Disease Frequency",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A local health department calculates the number of new cases of tuberculosis over the past year per 100,000 population. What measure is being reported and why is it useful for public health planning?",
      "choices": [
        "Incidence rate; it reflects the risk of developing disease and informs resource allocation and control measures",
        "Prevalence; it tracks cumulative lifetime burden",
        "Mortality rate; it indicates treatment success only",
        "Case fatality ratio; it measures exposure risk"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Incidence rate measures new cases over a time period per population at risk, useful for assessing transmission dynamics and planning interventions; prevalence measures existing cases at a time point.",
      "trap": "Prevalence includes both new and existing cases and is influenced by disease duration.",
      "related": "Incidence informs effectiveness of prevention programs and outbreak detection.",
      "tags": ["incidence","epidemiology","public-health"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PH2023002",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Biostatistics",
      "topic": "Sensitivity and Specificity",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A new screening test for cervical cancer has high sensitivity but moderate specificity. In a low-prevalence population, what is the likely impact on positive predictive value (PPV) and false positives, and how should this influence screening policy?",
      "choices": [
        "PPV will be low and false positives numerous; screening in low-prevalence settings may require confirmatory testing to avoid overtreatment",
        "PPV will be high and false positives rare regardless of prevalence",
        "Specificity does not affect PPV",
        "High sensitivity ensures no follow-up testing is needed"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "PPV depends on prevalence; high sensitivity with moderate specificity in low-prevalence populations yields low PPV and many false positives, necessitating confirmatory diagnostics to prevent harm.",
      "trap": "Assuming high PPV irrespective of prevalence is incorrect; both test characteristics and prevalence shape predictive values.",
      "related": "Screening policies balance sensitivity, specificity, prevalence, and harms of false positives.",
      "tags": ["sensitivity","specificity","predictive-value"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Health Policy",
      "topic": "Vaccination Programs",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A public health clinic plans an influenza vaccination campaign targeting frail elderly in long-term care facilities. What strategy best promotes high uptake and why?",
      "choices": [
        "On-site vaccination clinics with standing orders and opt-out consent to improve convenience and reduce barriers",
        "Passive education only expecting self-arranged vaccination",
        "Restricting vaccine to hospital pharmacies requiring prescription",
        "Offer vaccination only during off-site community fairs"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "On-site clinics with standing orders increase accessibility and streamline delivery, addressing logistical barriers and improving vaccination rates among high-risk groups.",
      "trap": "Relying on passive education or requiring prescriptions reduces uptake.",
      "related": "Multimodal strategies including reminders and provider recommendation further enhance coverage.",

      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Infectious Disease Control",
      "topic": "Contact Tracing",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "In an outbreak of measles with high R0, rapid contact tracing and isolation is initiated. What is the public health rationale for early contact tracing and which metric determines the intensity of response needed?",
      "choices": [
        "Interrupt transmission chains by identifying and isolating contacts; the basic reproductive number (R0) and effective reproductive number (Rt) guide response intensity",
        "Only individual case treatment is sufficient; attack rate is irrelevant",
        "Contact tracing is not useful for high R0 infections",
        "Use contact tracing only after outbreak resolves"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Contact tracing aims to identify exposed persons to prevent onward transmission; R0/Rt quantify transmissibility and inform urgency and scale of public health measures.",
      "trap": "High R0 increases required coverage and speed of contact tracing to control spread.",
      "related": "Measles's high R0 requires rapid and comprehensive public health action including vaccination campaigns.",

      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },

      "id": "PH2023005",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Environmental Health",
      "topic": "Air Pollution",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A city reports increased emergency visits for asthma exacerbations during a wildfire event. Which particulate matter size is most associated with deeper lung penetration and increased respiratory morbidity, and what mitigation advice reduces exposure?",
      "choices": [
        "PM2.5 (particles ≤2.5 μm); stay indoors with high-efficiency filtration and limit strenuous outdoor activity",
        "PM10 only affects skin and not lungs",
        "Large particles (>10 μm) penetrate deepest causing severe asthma",
        "Ultrafine particles do not affect respiratory health"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "PM2.5 penetrates deep into alveoli causing inflammation and exacerbations; public health advice includes staying indoors, using HEPA filtration, and avoiding outdoor exertion during high pollution.",
      "trap": "Larger particles deposit in upper airways and are less associated with alveolar disease; ultrafine particles also can be harmful but PM2.5 is the standard metric.",
      "related": "Air quality indices guide public advisories during wildfire smoke events.",
      "tags": ["air-pollution","pm2.5","environmental-health"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PH2023006",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Biostatistics",
      "topic": "Study Design",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A community trial randomizes neighborhoods to a sanitation intervention with pre- and post-measurement of diarrheal incidence. What study design is this and what bias is especially of concern?",
      "choices": [
        "Cluster randomized trial; risk of contamination and intracluster correlation affecting sample size and analysis",
        "Case-control study; recall bias is main issue only",
        "Cohort study without randomization eliminating confounding definitively",
        "Cross-sectional study capturing incidence accurately over time"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Randomizing groups (neighborhoods) is a cluster RCT; intracluster correlation reduces effective sample size and contamination between clusters can bias estimates—analysis must account for clustering.",
      "trap": "Recall bias is a concern in case-control studies but cluster RCTs have unique clustering/contamination issues.",
      "related": "Design choices impact power calculations and interpretation of community interventions.",
      "tags": ["cluster-randomized","study-design","bias"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PH2023007",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Health Systems",
      "topic": "Universal Health Coverage",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A low-income country aims to implement universal health coverage (UHC). Which core dimensions must be balanced in UHC policy and what is a common trade-off for rapid scale-up?",
      "choices": [
        "Population coverage, service coverage, and financial protection; trade-offs may include limiting service scope for rapid expansion",
        "Only financial protection matters; services can be ignored",
        "UHC requires privatization to succeed universally",
        "Trade-offs are irrelevant when implementing UHC"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "UHC requires balancing who is covered, which services are provided, and the level of financial protection (affordability); rapid scale-up often requires prioritization of essential service packages.",
      "trap": "Assuming UHC can cover everything immediately ignores resource constraints and necessary prioritization.",
      "related": "Health policy choices shape equity, access, and sustainability of UHC.",
      "tags": ["uhc","health-policy","universal-coverage"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PH2023008",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Screening",
      "topic": "Screening Test Evaluation",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": true,
      "stem": "A new cancer screening shows a lead-time bias concern. What is lead-time bias and how can investigators adjust survival comparisons to account for it?",
      "choices": [
        "Lead-time bias is the apparent increase in survival time due solely to earlier detection without altering disease course; use mortality rates or randomized controlled trials to assess true benefit",
        "Lead-time bias refers to delayed diagnosis improving survival",
        "Lead-time bias increases specificity of screening tests",
        "Adjusting for lead-time bias requires ignoring all early detections"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Lead-time bias artificially inflates survival time from diagnosis by earlier detection without changing time of death; true benefit is assessed by reduction in disease-specific mortality or RCTs rather than survival since diagnosis.",
      "trap": "Misinterpreting longer survival after screening as proof of benefit ignores lead-time bias.",
      "related": "Overdiagnosis bias is a related screening artifact requiring careful interpretation of screening outcomes.",
      "tags": ["lead-time-bias","screening","mortality"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PH2023009",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Occupational Health",
      "topic": "Exposure Assessment",
      "difficulty": "Medium",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "Workers exposed to silica dust have increased risk of silicosis and tuberculosis. Which workplace control is most effective to reduce inhalational exposure and disease risk?",
      "choices": [
        "Engineering controls (wet methods, local exhaust ventilation) combined with respiratory protection reduce airborne silica exposure",
        "Rely on hand hygiene alone to prevent inhalation",
        "Administrative controls are unnecessary if PPE is used inconsistently",
        "Only medical surveillance without exposure reduction prevents disease"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Engineering controls to reduce airborne particulates are the most effective primary prevention; PPE and administrative controls supplement but eliminating exposure at source is paramount.",
      "trap": "Medical surveillance cannot substitute for exposure control; hand hygiene is irrelevant for inhalational hazards.",
      "related": "Silica exposure control also lowers TB co-risk and other respiratory morbidity.",
      "tags": ["silica","engineering-controls","occupational-health"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    },
    {
      "id": "PH2023010",
      "subject": "Public Health",
      "chapter": "Health Promotion",
      "topic": "Behavior Change",
      "difficulty": "Hard",
      "highYield": false,
      "stem": "A smoking cessation program uses motivational interviewing and nicotine replacement therapy. Which behavioral theory underpins motivational interviewing and how does combining pharmacologic and behavioral interventions improve outcomes?",
      "choices": [
        "Motivational interviewing is based on stages-of-change/transtheoretical model promoting intrinsic motivation; combining therapy addresses both physiological dependence and behavioral drivers increasing quit rates",
        "Motivational interviewing is purely educational without behavior change theory",
        "Pharmacologic therapy alone yields maximal sustained cessation rates",
        "Behavioral interventions are ineffective and reduce pharmacologic efficacy"
      ],
      "answer": 0,
      "explanation": "Motivational interviewing aligns with the transtheoretical model facilitating progression through stages of change; combining pharmacologic (reducing withdrawal) with behavioral strategies (addressing habits and motivation) yields higher quit rates.",
      "trap": "Behavioral counseling is a critical adjunct, not merely education.",
      "related": "Comprehensive cessation programs include counseling, pharmacotherapy, and follow-up.",
      "tags": ["motivation","cessation","health-promotion"],
      "image": null,
      "reference": null
    }
  ]
}{
  "generator": {
    "name": "Multi-Discipline Exam Generator",
    "version": "2.0",
    "description": "Generates randomized mock exams (Mock001 .. Mock###) from multiple subject banks (physiology, histology, embryology, biochemistry, microbiology, immunology, parasitology, pathology, pharmacology, publichealth). Ensures per-exam no-duplicate questions, subject-distribution adherence, balanced difficulty within each exam, reproducible RNG seeds, and supports unlimited mock generation.",
    "input_banks": [
      "physiology.json",
      "histology.json",
      "embryology.json",
      "biochemistry.json",
      "microbiology.json",
      "immunology.json",
      "parasitology.json",
      "pathology.json",
      "pharmacology.json",
      "publichealth.json"
    ],
    "default_subject_distribution_percent": {
      "Physiology": 10,
      "Histology": 10,
      "Embryology": 10,
      "Biochemistry": 10,
      "Microbiology": 10,
      "Immunology": 10,
      "Parasitology": 10,
      "Pathology": 10,
      "Pharmacology": 10,
      "PublicHealth": 10
    },
    "default_difficulty_distribution_percent": {
      "Easy": 0,
      "Medium": 50,
      "Hard": 50
    },
    "default_questions_per_mock": 100,
    "mocks_to_generate_default": 100,
    "rules": {
      "randomize": "Questions and answer-choice order optionally randomized per mock; RNG seed recorded per mock enabling exact reproduction.",
      "no_duplicates_within_mock": "Strict — sampling without replacement within each mock.",
      "minimize_reuse_across_mocks": "Prefer unused questions when building a new mock until pool exhausted; when exhausted, allow reuse across mocks but never duplicate within a single mock.",
      "subject_allocation": "Per-mock subject counts computed from distribution percentages using largest-remainder rounding so that total equals questions_per_mock.",
      "difficulty_balance": "Within each subject allocation, pick questions to approximate overall difficulty distribution using proportional allocation and nearest-available substitution when exact counts unavailable.",
      "conflict_handling": "If bank lacks sufficient questions for requested subject/difficulty allocation, the generator compensates by (1) selecting from other difficulties in same subject, (2) selecting from other subjects with related topics, and (3) issuing warnings in log if shortages forced.",
      "reproducibility": "Each mock records a unique seed; regenerate with same seed and unchanged bank yields identical mock."
    },
    "outputs": {
      "mock_file_format": "JSON",
      "mock_filename_template": "Mock{index:03d}.json",
      "mock_file_contents": {
        "metadata": ["mock_id", "seed", "questions_per_mock", "subject_counts", "difficulty_allocation", "creation_timestamp"],
        "question_list": "Array of full question objects by default (configurable to output only IDs to reduce file size)"
      },
      "index_file": "mocks_index.json (list of generated mock metadata and file paths)",
      "log_file": "generator.log (warnings, shortages, runtime summary)"
    }
  },
  "usage": {
    "summary": "Place all question bank JSON files (named as in input_banks) in same folder as the generator script. Configure questions_per_mock, mocks_to_generate, and distributions if desired. Run the script; it will output Mock001.json ... Mock100.json (or more) in the output directory.",
    "notes": [
      "Default per-mock question count is 100; adjust to smaller size if your aggregate bank is small to reduce repeated reuse across many mocks.",
      "Default subject distribution is equal across the 10 listed subjects. Edit default_subject_distribution_percent to match your official distribution if different.",
      "Difficulty categories expected in question objects: 'Easy', 'Medium', 'Hard'. If a question lacks difficulty, it will be treated as 'Medium' by default."
    ]
  },
  "python_generator": "'''\nExam generator script (Python 3.8+).\nSaves Mock###.json files and mocks_index.json in output directory.\nConfiguration variables near top can be adjusted.\n''' \nimport json, os, math, random, time\nfrom collections import defaultdict, Counter\n\n# ---------- Configuration ----------\nINPUT_BANK_FILES = [\n    'physiology.json', 'histology.json', 'embryology.json', 'biochemistry.json',\n    'microbiology.json', 'immunology.json', 'parasitology.json', 'pathology.json',\n    'pharmacology.json', 'publichealth.json'\n]\nOUTPUT_DIR = 'generated_mocks'\nQUESTIONS_PER_MOCK = 100\nMOCKS_TO_GENERATE = 100\n# Official subject distribution (edit if official differs)\nSUBJECT_DISTRIBUTION = {\n    'Physiology': 10,\n    'Histology': 10,\n    'Embryology': 10,\n    'Biochemistry': 10,\n    'Microbiology': 10,\n    'Immunology': 10,\n    'Parasitology': 10,\n    'Pathology': 10,\n    'Pharmacology': 10,\n    'PublicHealth': 10\n}\nDIFFICULTY_DISTRIBUTION = {'Easy': 0, 'Medium': 50, 'Hard': 50}\nPREFER_MINIMIZE_REUSE_ACROSS_MOCKS = True\nINCLUDE_FULL_QUESTIONS_IN_OUTPUT = True  # Set False to output only question IDs (smaller files)\n# -----------------------------------\n\n# Utility: largest-remainder allocation\ndef allocate_counts(total, distribution):\n    labels = list(distribution.keys())\n    floats = {l: total * distribution[l] / 100.0 for l in labels}\n    ints = {l: int(math.floor(floats[l])) for l in labels}\n    remainder = total - sum(ints.values())\n    frac_sorted = sorted(labels, key=lambda l: floats[l] - ints[l], reverse=True)\n    for i in range(remainder):\n        ints[frac_sorted[i]] += 1\n    return ints\n\n# Load all banks and index by Subject and Difficulty\nquestion_bank = []\nid_to_question = {}\nsubject_index = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list))  # subject -> difficulty -> [ids]\nall_subjects_present = set()\n\nfor fname in INPUT_BANK_FILES:\n    if not os.path.isfile(fname):\n        # skip missing files but warn\n        print(f'Warning: input bank {fname} not found; skipping')\n        continue\n    with open(fname, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n        items = json.load(f)\n        for q in items:\n            qid = q.get('id')\n            subj = q.get('subject') or q.get('chapter') or 'Unknown'\n            # Normalize subject name to keys in SUBJECT_DISTRIBUTION if possible\n            subj_norm = subj.strip()\n            # Map case-insensitive to provided SUBJECT_DISTRIBUTION keys\n            matched = None\n            for key in SUBJECT_DISTRIBUTION.keys():\n                if subj_norm.lower() == key.lower() or subj_norm.lower() == key.replace(' ', '').lower():\n                    matched = key\n                    break\n            if matched:\n                subj_key = matched\n            else:\n                # Try simple title-case mapping\n                subj_key = subj_norm.title()\n            diff = q.get('difficulty') or 'Medium'\n            if diff not in ('Easy', 'Medium', 'Hard'):\n                diff = 'Medium'\n            if qid in id_to_question:\n                # Ensure unique IDs across banks by appending suffix if collision (rare)\n                suffix = 2\n                new_id = f\"{qid}_{suffix}\"\n                while new_id in id_to_question:\n                    suffix += 1\n                    new_id = f\"{qid}_{suffix}\"\n                qid = new_id\n                q['id'] = qid\n            id_to_question[qid] = q\n            subject_index[subj_key][diff].append(qid)\n            all_subjects_present.add(subj_key)\n\nall_qids = list(id_to_question.keys())\nprint(f'Total questions loaded: {len(all_qids)} across subjects: {sorted(list(all_subjects_present))}')\n\n# Prepare unused pool for minimizing reuse across mocks\nunused_pool = set(all_qids)\nglobal_usage = Counter()\n\nos.makedirs(OUTPUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)\nindex_list = []\nlog_lines = []\n\nfor mock_num in range(1, MOCKS_TO_GENERATE + 1):\n    mock_id = f'Mock{mock_num:03d}'\n    seed = random.SystemRandom().randint(1, 2**31 - 1)\n    rng = random.Random(seed)\n\n    # Determine subject counts\n    subj_counts = allocate_counts(QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK, SUBJECT_DISTRIBUTION)\n\n    # Determine overall difficulty allocation\n    diff_counts_total = allocate_counts(QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK, DIFFICULTY_DISTRIBUTION)\n\n    # Distribute difficulty counts across subjects proportionally to subject_counts\n    subj_diff_counts = {s: {d: 0 for d in diff_counts_total} for s in subj_counts}\n    for d in diff_counts_total:\n        remaining = diff_counts_total[d]\n        # proportional floats\n        floats = {s: subj_counts[s] * (diff_counts_total[d] / QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK) for s in subj_counts}\n        ints = {s: int(math.floor(floats[s])) for s in subj_counts}\n        rem = remaining - sum(ints.values())\n        frac_sorted = sorted(subj_counts.keys(), key=lambda s: floats[s] - ints[s], reverse=True)\n        for i in range(rem):\n            ints[frac_sorted[i]] += 1\n        for s in subj_counts:\n            subj_diff_counts[s][d] = ints[s]\n\n    selected_qids = []\n    shortages = []\n\n    # For each subject/difficulty, try to select required number\n    for s in subj_counts:\n        for d in subj_diff_counts[s]:\n            need = subj_diff_counts[s][d]\n            if need == 0:\n                continue\n            pool = list(subject_index.get(s, {}).get(d, []))\n            # Exclude qids already selected in this mock\n            pool = [q for q in pool if q not in selected_qids]\n            chosen = []\n            # Prefer unused across mocks\n            if PREFER_MINIMIZE_REUSE_ACROSS_MOCKS:\n                unused_in_pool = [q for q in pool if q in unused_pool]\n                take = min(len(unused_in_pool), need)\n                if take > 0:\n                    chosen.extend(rng.sample(unused_in_pool, take))\n                    need -= take\n            # Fill remaining from pool\n            if need > 0 and pool:\n                remaining_choices = [q for q in pool if q not in chosen]\n                take = min(len(remaining_choices), need)\n                if take > 0:\n                    chosen.extend(rng.sample(remaining_choices, take))\n                    need -= take\n            # If still need, try other difficulties in same subject\n            if need > 0:\n                other_ds = [od for od in subject_index.get(s, {}) if od != d]\n                for od in other_ds:\n                    pool2 = [q for q in subject_index[s][od] if q not in selected_qids and q not in chosen]\n                    take = min(len(pool2), need)\n                    if take > 0:\n                        chosen.extend(rng.sample(pool2, take))\n                        need -= take\n                    if need == 0:\n                        break\n            # If still need, draw from global pool (other subjects)\n            if need > 0:\n                global_pool = [q for q in all_qids if q not in selected_qids and q not in chosen]\n                if PREFER_MINIMIZE_REUSE_ACROSS_MOCKS:\n                    global_unused = [q for q in global_pool if q in unused_pool]\n                    take = min(len(global_unused), need)\n                    if take > 0:\n                        chosen.extend(rng.sample(global_unused, take))\n                        need -= take\n                    if need > 0:\n                        remaining_global = [q for q in global_pool if q not in chosen]\n                        take = min(len(remaining_global), need)\n                        if take > 0:\n                            chosen.extend(rng.sample(remaining_global, take))\n                            need -= take\n                else:\n                    take = min(len(global_pool), need)\n                    if take > 0:\n                        chosen.extend(rng.sample(global_pool, take))\n                        need -= take\n            if need > 0:\n                shortages.append({'subject': s, 'difficulty': d, 'shortage': need})\n            selected_qids.extend(chosen)\n\n    # If selected fewer than needed due to shortages, fill from any remaining qids (no duplicates within mock)\n    if len(selected_qids) < QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK:\n        remaining_needed = QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK - len(selected_qids)\n        remaining_pool = [q for q in all_qids if q not in selected_qids]\n        if len(remaining_pool) < remaining_needed:\n            # allow reuse across mocks (but not within this mock) by replenishing remaining_pool to all_qids excluding selected\n            remaining_pool = [q for q in all_qids if q not in selected_qids]\n        if remaining_pool:\n            take = min(len(remaining_pool), remaining_needed)\n            selected_qids.extend(rng.sample(remaining_pool, take))\n            if take < remaining_needed:\n                shortages.append({'fill_remaining': remaining_needed - take})\n\n    # Shuffle final selection for randomness\n    rng.shuffle(selected_qids)\n\n    # Update usage and unused pool\n    for qid in selected_qids:\n        global_usage[qid] += 1\n        unused_pool.discard(qid)\n    # If unused pool gets very small, reset to allow continued generation while still minimizing reuse\n    if PREFER_MINIMIZE_REUSE_ACROSS_MOCKS and len(unused_pool) < QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK // 2:\n        unused_pool = set([q for q in all_qids if global_usage[q] == 0])\n        if not unused_pool:\n            unused_pool = set(all_qids)\n\n    # Prepare mock object\n    mock_obj = {\n        'mock_id': mock_id,\n        'seed': seed,\n        'created_at': time.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ', time.gmtime()),\n        'questions_per_mock': QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK,\n        'subject_counts': subj_counts,\n        'difficulty_allocation_total': diff_counts_total,\n        'shortages': shortages,\n        'questions': []\n    }\n    if INCLUDE_FULL_QUESTIONS_IN_OUTPUT:\n        mock_obj['questions'] = [id_to_question[qid] for qid in selected_qids]\n    else:\n        mock_obj['question_ids'] = selected_qids\n\n    out_path = os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, f'{mock_id}.json')\n    with open(out_path, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as fout:\n        json.dump(mock_obj, fout, indent=2)\n\n    index_list.append({'mock_id': mock_id, 'file': out_path, 'seed': seed, 'n_questions': QUESTIONS_PER_MOCK, 'shortages': shortages})\n    log_lines.append(f'Generated {mock_id} seed={seed} shortages={shortages}')\n    print(f'Generated {mock_id} ({len(selected_qids)} questions)')\n\n# Write index and log\nwith open(os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, 'mocks_index.json'), 'w', encoding='utf-8') as ix:\n    json.dump(index_list, ix, indent=2)\nwith open(os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, 'generator.log'), 'w', encoding='utf-8') as lg:\n    lg.write('\\n'.join(log_lines))\n\nprint('Generation complete. Index and mock files are in', OUTPUT_DIR)\n",
  "example_output_metadata": {
    "mocks_generated": 100,
    "questions_per_mock": 100,
    "subject_distribution_used": "Equal 10% per listed subject (modifiable)",
    "difficulty_balance_used": "50% Medium, 50% Hard (modifiable)",
    "reproducibility": "Each mock file contains 'seed' for exact regeneration"
  },
  "final_notes": {
    "next_steps": [
      "If you want, I can (A) run this generator here and output Mock001..Mock100 JSON files (requires me to include all question bank content in the response and will produce a large JSON payload), or (B) you can run the provided script locally against the question bank files you already have (recommended for large-scale generation).",
      "If you want a different official subject distribution or a different number of questions per mock, tell me and I will update the configuration accordingly."
    ]
  }
}{
  "adaptive_learning_system": {
    "name": "Adaptive Learning Engine",
    "version": "1.0",
    "architecture": "Client-side JavaScript (LocalStorage), no backend required",
    "description": "Tracks user performance across questions, identifies weak topics and misconceptions, and adaptively recommends next questions with increased probability for areas needing reinforcement.",
    "core_modules": [
      "performance_tracker",
      "question_recommender",
      "weak_topic_detector",
      "misconception_analyzer",
      "bookmark_manager",
      "local_storage_handler"
    ],
    "implementation": {
      "language": "JavaScript (ES6+)",
      "storage": "Browser LocalStorage (5-50MB depending on browser)",
      "no_backend": true,
      "real_time_tracking": true
    }
  },
  "localstorage_schema": {
    "user_profile": {
      "key": "adaptive_user_profile",
      "structure": {
        "user_id": "uuid or timestamp-based",
        "created_at": "ISO 8601 timestamp",
        "total_questions_attempted": "number",
        "overall_accuracy": "percent (0-100)",
        "average_speed_seconds": "number"
      }
    },
    "question_history": {
      "key": "adaptive_question_history",
      "structure": {
        "entries": [
          {
            "question_id": "string",
            "mock_id": "string",
            "subject": "string",
            "chapter": "string",
            "topic": "string",
            "difficulty": "string (Easy/Medium/Hard)",
            "correct": "boolean",
            "time_taken_seconds": "number",
            "timestamp": "ISO 8601",
            "user_answer_index": "number",
            "correct_answer_index": "number",
            "bookmarked": "boolean",
            "notes": "string (optional)"
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    "subject_stats": {
      "key": "adaptive_subject_stats",
      "structure": {
        "stats_by_subject": {
          "[subject_name]": {
            "total_attempted": "number",
            "total_correct": "number",
            "accuracy_percent": "number",
            "average_time_seconds": "number",
            "difficulty_distribution": {
              "Easy": { "attempted": "number", "correct": "number" },
              "Medium": { "attempted": "number", "correct": "number" },
              "Hard": { "attempted": "number", "correct": "number" }
            },
            "trending_difficulty": "string (improves/stagnant/declines)",
            "last_practiced": "ISO 8601"
          }
        }
      }
    },
    "topic_stats": {
      "key": "adaptive_topic_stats",
      "structure": {
        "stats_by_topic": {
          "[subject/topic]": {
            "total_attempted": "number",
            "total_correct": "number",
            "accuracy_percent": "number",
            "misconceptions": [
              {
                "concept": "string",
                "error_count": "number",
                "last_seen": "ISO 8601",
                "example_question_ids": ["string"]
              }
            ],
            "strength_level": "string (very_weak/weak/average/strong/very_strong)",
            "priority_rank": "number (1=highest priority for practice)"
          }
        }
      }
    },
    "weak_areas": {
      "key": "adaptive_weak_areas",
      "structure": {
        "weak_subjects": [
          {
            "subject": "string",
            "accuracy_percent": "number",
            "rank": "number (1=weakest)"
          }
        ],
        "weak_topics": [
          {
            "topic": "string",
            "accuracy_percent": "number",
            "times_attempted": "number",
            "last_error_timestamp": "ISO 8601",
            "priority_boost_factor": "number (1.5-3.0 for question recommender)"
          }
        ],
        "misconceptions": [
          {
            "id": "uuid",
            "question_id": "string",
            "concept": "string",
            "typical_wrong_answer": "number (choice index)",
            "correct_answer": "number (choice index)",
            "frequency": "number (how many times user picked wrong answer)",
            "tags": ["string"]
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    "bookmarks": {
      "key": "adaptive_bookmarks",
      "structure": {
        "bookmarked_questions": [
          {
            "question_id": "string",
            "mock_id": "string",
            "reason": "string (difficult/confusing/important/review_later/common_error)",
            "created_at": "ISO 8601",
            "tags": ["string"],
            "personal_notes": "string"
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    "learning_plan": {
      "key": "adaptive_learning_plan",
      "structure": {
        "recommended_queue": [
          {
            "question_id": "string",
            "priority_score": "number (0-100)",
            "reason": "string (weak_topic/misconception_review/progressive_difficulty/bookmarked/weak_subject)",
            "recommended_at": "ISO 8601",
            "attempt_count": "number"
          }
        ],
        "next_recommended_question_id": "string",
        "recommendation_updated_at": "ISO 8601"
      }
    }
  },
  "javascript_implementation": {
    "file": "adaptive_learning.js",
    "classes": [
      "AdaptiveUserProfile",
      "PerformanceTracker",
      "WeakTopicDetector",
      "MisconceptionAnalyzer",
      "QuestionRecommender",
      "BookmarkManager",
      "LocalStorageHandler"
    ],
    "code": "/**\n * Adaptive Learning Engine\n * Tracks performance, identifies weak areas, and recommends questions adaptively\n * All data stored in browser LocalStorage (no backend)\n */\n\nclass LocalStorageHandler {\n  constructor(prefix = 'adaptive_') {\n    this.prefix = prefix;\n  }\n\n  set(key, value) {\n    try {\n      localStorage.setItem(this.prefix + key, JSON.stringify(value));\n    } catch (e) {\n      console.error('LocalStorage quota exceeded:', e);\n    }\n  }\n\n  get(key, defaultValue = null) {\n    const item = localStorage.getItem(this.prefix + key);\n    if (!item) return defaultValue;\n    try {\n      return JSON.parse(item);\n    } catch (e) {\n      console.error('Error parsing LocalStorage item:', e);\n      return defaultValue;\n    }\n  }\n\n  delete(key) {\n    localStorage.removeItem(this.prefix + key);\n  }\n\n  clear() {\n    const keys = Object.keys(localStorage);\n    keys.forEach(k => {\n      if (k.startsWith(this.prefix)) {\n
